Starting a new role as a Product Manager can feel like being handed the keys to a moving car. There’s pressure to deliver quickly, but real impact comes from learning the business, earning trust, and making informed decisions. A thoughtful 30-60-90-day plan helps balance urgency with effectiveness.
Here’s what a strong first-90-day plan should look like for a Product Manager joining a new organization.
Days 1–30: Learn, Listen, and Build Context
The first month is about understanding before acting. Your primary job is to absorb information and establish credibility through curiosity and clarity.
Focus areas:
• Understand the company’s mission, strategy, and success metrics
• Learn the product portfolio, customers, and market positioning
• Build relationships with engineering, design, sales, marketing, and leadership
• Get familiar with existing roadmaps, backlogs, and delivery processes
Key activities:
• Conduct stakeholder interviews to understand expectations and pain points
• Review customer feedback, support tickets, and sales insights
• Study analytics, KPIs, and financial drivers tied to the product
• Observe team rituals and decision-making norms
Outcomes by Day 30:
• Clear understanding of the product’s purpose and value proposition
• A map of key stakeholders and decision influencers
• Identified gaps, risks, and opportunities (without prescribing solutions yet)
Days 31–60: Analyze, Align, and Prioritize
The second phase is about turning knowledge into direction. You should begin forming opinions, validating assumptions, and aligning stakeholders around priorities.
Focus areas:
• Evaluate the current product strategy and roadmap
• Identify quick wins and high-impact opportunities
• Clarify roles, ownership, and decision frameworks
Key activities:
• Validate problems using data and customer insights
Recommended by LinkedIn
Level Up Your Product Game: A 90-Day Plan
Vinai Mudaliar   1 year ago
The Science and The Art of Prioritization and…
Vittal K - MCA, MS, Executive MBA   1 year ago
Why Great Product Managers Don’t Chase Features — They…
Dishant Salunke   5 months ago
• Pressure-test the roadmap against business goals and user needs
• Collaborate with engineering to understand technical constraints and velocity
• Begin refining the backlog with clearer outcomes and acceptance criteria
Outcomes by Day 60:
• A prioritized view of what matters most and why
• Alignment with leadership on near-term goals
• Improved clarity for the delivery team on priorities and success metrics
Days 61–90: Execute, Influence, and Drive Outcomes
The final phase is about delivering value and demonstrating leadership. By now, you should be trusted enough to make decisions and move initiatives forward.
Focus areas:
• Execute against agreed priorities
• Improve product processes and communication
• Establish a forward-looking product narrative
Key activities:
• Launch or advance key initiatives tied to measurable outcomes
• Introduce improvements to discovery, planning, or feedback loops
• Communicate progress and trade-offs transparently
• Set a clear 6–12-month product vision or roadmap
Outcomes by Day 90:
• Tangible product progress with measurable impact
• Strong working relationships and decision credibility
• A clear, shared understanding of where the product is headed
A strong 30-60-90-day plan is not a checklist or a race to ship features. It is a deliberate progression from understanding to influence to impact. The most effective Product Managers resist the urge to “fix” things too quickly and instead focus on building shared context, aligning incentives, and grounding decisions in real customer and business signals.
By the end of the first 90 days, success is not measured by how much you changed, but by how clearly the organization understands its product direction, how confidently teams can execute, and how well trade-offs are made. Trust, clarity, and credibility compound over time—and the groundwork for all three is set in those first few months.
When approached with intention, the first 90 days don’t just onboard a Product Manager; they establish a product leader.

How to Create a 30/60/90-Day Plan: A Guide for New Product Managers
How-to-Develop-a-30-60-90-Day-Plan-banner1
“Setting goals is the first step in turning the invisible into the visible.” This quote by Tony Robbins perfectly captures the power of goal-setting, especially when stepping into a new product leadership role.
As a new product leader, aligning vision with execution is key, and a 30/60/90-day plan provides the structure needed to bridge the gap between your starting point and your desired outcome.
This strategic framework helps new product leaders set clear, actionable goals, assess team strengths and opportunities for improvement, and prioritize key initiatives from day one. With a solid plan, new product managers can quickly align with the product vision, collaborate with stakeholders, and refine the roadmap based on user feedback and market insights.
This guide will walk you through the process of developing your plan step by step, providing clear examples, templates, and practical tips to help you navigate the first three months of your product leadership journey.
What is a 30/60/90-day plan, and why is it important for new product managers?
A 30/60/90-day plan is a strategic tool that helps new product managers outline key goals and priorities for their first three months in a role. It provides a clear framework for adjusting to the position, managing early tasks, and delivering impactful results.
The plan is divided into three phases:
For example, a new product manager might spend the first 30 days understanding the product’s market position, meeting key stakeholders, and gathering customer feedback. By the 60-day mark, they could collaborate with teams to refine the roadmap and address process gaps. By the 90-day phase, they might launch a pilot feature based on feedback, measure its success, and present a strategy for scaling the product to senior product leadership.
A 30/60/90-day plan is especially valuable for new leaders in product management. It accelerates the acclimation process, prioritizes tasks effectively, and ensures actions align with broader organizational goals. The framework provides focus during each phase, helping new product managers gain credibility, drive improvements, and deliver lasting results.
The components of a 30/60/90-day plan
Creating an effective 30/60/90-day plan requires several key components to ensure a structured and strategic approach:
| Component | Description | How It Fits Into the Plan |
 --- 
| Self-Assessment and Goal Setting | Define your role, identify key stakeholders, and set SMART goals for each phase. | Provides clarity on responsibilities and aligns efforts with organizational priorities. |
| Learning and Adaptation | Understand the product, market, and target audience. Attend trainings or workshops as needed. | Builds a solid foundation of knowledge, enabling informed decision-making in later phases. |
| Prioritization and Task Management | Focus on high-impact tasks, break them into actionable steps, and use time management techniques. | Ensures key objectives are executed efficiently, driving meaningful results. |
| Collaboration and Communication | Build relationships with stakeholders and communicate progress regularly. | Strengthens alignment and fosters teamwork, ensuring collective goal achievement. |
| Measurement and Evaluation | Track progress with KPIs, adjust the plan as necessary and regularly assess achievements. | Keeps the plan on track and aligned with long-term goals, ensuring continual progress. |
These components ensure your 30/60/90-day plan is comprehensive, purposeful, and adaptable. They will guide you through the first three months and set the stage for long-term success.
3 key steps to develop your 30/60/90 day plan: Best practices and overcoming challenges
To successfully navigate your first 90 days as a product leader, you must break down your plan into three distinct phases: 30, 60, and 90 days. Each phase has its own focus, and understanding the best practices and challenges specific to each step will help you stay on track and drive impactful results from day one.
Step 1: Developing your 30-day plan
In the first 30 days, your focus should be on learning and observation. Spend time understanding your team’s dynamics, familiarizing yourself with key stakeholders, and building foundational relationships. Conduct a SWOT analysis to assess the current state of the team and the product. Set initial goals based on these observations and prioritize gaining insights over immediate changes.
Step 2: Developing your 60-day plan
During the 60-day phase, begin implementing improvements. Work closely with team members to enhance performance, align with stakeholders on key objectives, and set measurable KPIs. Continue refining your understanding of the organization’s goals and your role within them while taking action on areas identified for improvement.
Step 3: Developing your 90-day plan
In the final 30 days, shift your focus to driving strategic initiatives and evaluating the progress you’ve made. Strengthen your product leadership presence and look ahead by planning for the future. Use the insights and feedback gathered to refine your product strategy for the team and establish a sustainable plan for ongoing growth.
By following these steps, best practices, and strategies for overcoming challenges, you’ll be well-equipped to lead effectively and drive meaningful progress. With a clear 30/60/90 day plan in place, you can confidently navigate your new role and set the stage for long-term success.
Tools and resources for creating your 30/60/90 day plan
The right tools and resources can make executing your 30/60/90-day plan much more efficient and effective. Consider these options to help you stay organized and aligned with your goals:
Free online templates
Accessible templates provide a solid foundation that can be easily customized to fit your specific needs.
Task management tools
Tools like Asana, Trello, Monday.com, and Jira (or other Atlassian products) help with task organization, tracking deadlines, and team collaboration.
Mentorship
Seeking guidance from experienced product managers or joining industry networks can provide valuable insights and help you avoid common pitfalls.
These tools and resources can streamline your progress and support your product leadership development throughout the 30/60/90-day journey.
Evaluating, adjusting, and learning from your plan
As you progress through each phase of your 30/60/90 day plan, regular evaluation is key to ensuring alignment with organizational priorities. Set intervals to review your goals, track performance metrics, and assess whether adjustments are needed. This allows you to stay on course and make data-driven decisions.
Soliciting feedback from your team and stakeholders is equally important. Engaging with them will provide insights into what’s working and where improvements are necessary. This iterative process helps you refine your plan in real time.
While progress is often smooth, setbacks can occur. When things don’t go as expected, reflect on what went wrong. Analyze any underlying issues and use this as a growth opportunity. By staying adaptable and learning from challenges, you can continuously improve your approach and drive long-term success.
Conclusion
A well-crafted 30/60/90-day plan is not just a blueprint for your first three months—it’s a foundational tool for long-term product leadership success. By setting clear goals, staying adaptable, and committing to continuous learning, you position yourself for immediate wins while building the skills and relationships that will have a lasting impact.
Mastering this framework cultivates strategic thinking, resilience, and adaptability—key qualities for thriving in today’s fast-paced business world. As you refine your product management style and tackle future challenges, the lessons learned from this plan will empower you to lead with confidence, inspire your team, and deliver meaningful results.
Embrace this journey as a growth opportunity, knowing that each phase contributes to the lasting legacy you’ll create in your new role and beyond.

Your First 30, 60, 90 Days as a Product Manager [Checklist]: Tips & Tricks for Success in Your New Role
Product Management
Anthony PellegrinoAnthony Pellegrino • Last updated

Your first 90 days as a product manager can feel overwhelming. A commonly repeated phrase is that it feels like you're drinking from a firehose.
Many of the resources we have here at Exponent are dedicated to nailing your tech interviews so you can ultimately receive an offer. Now, you've made it through the product manager interview path successfully and it's time to get to work!
But what should you do in your first 30, 60, or 90 days as a product manager to set you up for success?
Your first quarter can really set the rhythm for many months (or years) to come.
Here's what you should do in your first 90 days as a product manager.
 📞 Talk to Users & Customers
 📱 Become Deeply Familiar With the Product
 📊 Get to Know the Stakeholders
 ❓ Ask Plenty of Questions/Learn As Much As You Can
 😀  Make a Good Impression Early
 ✅  Your First 30, 60, 90 Days as a PM Checklist
 Common Mistakes Made in Early Days as a PM
Talk to Users & Customers: Understand Their Pain Points

Abstract by Oleg Shcherba
Your first order of business as a new product manager should be talking to customers!
Customers are the lifeblood of your business. They should be the center of your attention when you start a new role.
At the end of the day, customers are your rock. They influence everything you'll create and ship as a successful product manager.
How to Schedule Customer Discovery Meetings
Talking to customers doesn't necessarily mean inviting them to the office for a focus group. Instead, you can talk to customers in these ways:
 Send an email to your mailing list asking for their time in exchange for a discount or gift card
 Deliver pop-ups to logged-in users asking them to participate in a call
 Run re-targeting ads on Google and Facebook with a call-to-action asking them to participate in a call
Talk to Coworkers About Their User Knowledge
New PMs should speak with fellow co-workers with deep user knowledge.
Community managers, sales teams, marketing managers, and more could provide much insight into a product's users.
You should try to learn about the following in your first 90 days as a PM:
 Customer segmentation and user personas;
 What pain points or problems your product attempts to solve;
 How your product impacts your customers;
 What are the potential weaknesses or shortcomings of the product;
 What user pain points/problems are not accounted for by product features.
Become Deeply Familiar With the Product by Using It

Abstract by Natasha Remarchuk
New PMs should do as much as they can in their first 90 days to become deeply familiar with their product(s).
You can do this by creating good habits in your first 30 days on the job. That means you have to download the app or sign up for the product!
In these first few months, you should spend as much time as you can using your product.
The best PMs understand their products from the bottom up.
Understand what it means to be a new user and how they see your application.
Without this first-hand knowledge and understanding of your product, you won't be able to develop or improve your product as much as possible.
You should be sure to do the following in your first 90 days as a PM:
 Review all the documentation for your product. Be sure to study the product roadmap, development demos, and anything else that will further your product understanding;
 If available/possible, complete any product training your organization provides;
 Pretend to be a first-time user—create an account go through the onboarding process;
 Consult with the engineering teams regarding the technical architecture of your product;
 Become deeply familiar with the product vision and strategy. Be sure to learn how the vision and strategy are accounted for by the product roadmap;
 Know what metrics teams use to measure product success;
 Deeply understand the primary value proposition of your product, especially as it relates to competitors;
Get to Know the Stakeholders at Your Organization

Abstract by Oleg Shcherba
Another crucial step of a successful 90 days as a new PM is to meet with all the product stakeholders and cross-functional teams as soon as possible.
Product managers who advance their career understand that the entire organization is responsible for a product's success. Spend time getting to know people from all sorts of teams.
In many ways, product management is as much about people and relationships as it is about developing products.
It's likely you'll be invited to many meetings in your first weeks at your new company.
You won't understand everything going on and that's ok!
Try to get to know the people in the room and how they play a part in the bigger organization.
Work to schedule 1-on-1s or meetings with the following people in your first 90 days as a product manager
 Other product managers;
 Engineering teams;
 Sales representatives;
 Marketing managers;
 Community managers;
 Program managers;
Many new PMs may neglect these meetings, thinking that they'll occur naturally. But, remember, first impressions are everything. Considering you will be working with these teams extensively during your tenure, these first impressions must be positive and productive.
Ask Plenty of Questions
You'll likely need some time in your first months as a new PM to get acquainted with everything before you can make any development moves. There's nothing wrong with this; in fact, it would be worrisome if you went in guns blazing without any understanding of the product (more on this later).
As is the case with everything in life, the best way to learn about anything is by asking questions, especially to those with more experience than you.
When it comes to your first days as a product manager, there are two primary things you should learn as much as you can about. These are the product's market/competitors and the product management processes that exist at the company.
Let's look at each in more detail:
Product Market & Competitive Analysis
We mentioned that new PMs should spend some of their earliest days learning as much as possible about their users. However, this shouldn't be the only aspect of their product they should be researching.
It's also critical for product managers to understand the market their products fit into along with their major competitors. Be sure to ask some of the following questions during your first 90 days:
 What are the primary competitors? What makes their product(s) different? How does our value proposition compare to theirs?
 What are the overlaps in customers that we share with our primary competitors?
 What are the limitations of our competitor's products? How does our product measure up?
How Does Product Management Work at Your Organization?
Learn as much as you can about is the product management processes and procedures at your company.
Every organization has a different company culture and way they get things done.
You'll find that some companies may rely heavily on particular frameworks like Agile or Waterfall, whereas others won't use them at all.
As such, new PMs should learn as much as they can about the specifics of product management at their new companies.
Ask the following questions in your first 90 days as a PM:
 How do product management teams develop roadmaps and determine priorities? How do PM teams manage their backlog?
 How are bugs handled? How are new features devised and developed?
 Where do new product ideas come from?
 Is it possible for customers to directly request features?
 How is product feedback processed by the company?
 What are the general challenges faced by the product management teams?
Make a Good Impression Early On

Abstract by Oleg Shcherba
Making a good first impression in your PM role will help you more easily navigate your product management career path.
Meeting with stakeholders isn't the only way to make a good impression early on.
New product managers also should set an example through their actions. Do your best to find something you can make a mark on in your early days.
Look for low-hanging fruit. Look for pain points that your PM team needs help addressing.
Making a good impression establishes your credibility as a PM. It also has the benefit of deepening relationships on your team.
The First 90 Days PM Checklist
Below, you'll find a checklist for your first 30, 60, and 90 days as a product manager.
Within Your First 30 Days as a Product Manager
 Begin using your product as much as you can;
 Schedule meetings with relevant stakeholders;
 Conduct research into users, competitors, and your target market;
 Become familiar with all necessary product metrics;
Within Your First 60 Days as a Product Manager
 Decide which short-term pieces of roadmap to prioritize;
 Meet with the head of Product Management at the company;
 Begin running product strategy meetings with your team(s);
 Determine what low hanging fruit can be accounted for;
Within Your First 90 Days as a Product Manager
 Choose a product feature that can be prioritized by your team;
 Begin shaping necessary product development processes with all relevant stakeholders;
 Ship your first feature or product function;
 Begin work on your next product development priority.
Download a PDF of our First 90 Day PM Checklist here.
Wanna know what a day in the life of a project manager is after 90 days? Read, What Does a Product Manager Do All Day? - A Day In the Life of a Product Manager.
Common Mistakes Made in Early Days as a PM
Don't Focus on the Micro
Many first-time product managers feel anxious in their first 90 days. They feel like they have impostor syndrome.
Changing careers to become a product manager may make it feel like you're a fish out of water. It's awkward to talk to new coworkers!
Many new PMs focus too much on things like paperwork or reading product documentation.
In other words, they spend too much time alone at their desks rather than interacting with others or making first impressions.
It's easy to see why new PMs would do this (it's not as scary)! But staying at your desk in your first few weeks could slow down your progress down the road!
You can't build relationships, learn about the company culture, or establish your credibility as a new product manager if you're glued to your desk.
Don't Change Everything Right Away
Another common mistake new PMs tend to make in their early days is coming in too hot. That is to say, they quickly make big declarations or rapidly push weighty product decisions.
Many new PMs make this mistake because they feel they need to overcompensate for their inexperience.
They're the new person at the company, and they need to make an impression quickly, right?
Yes, new product managers need to make a positive first impression. But the keyword there is positive.
If you come in guns blazin', you'll likely alienate or annoy your cross-functional teams.
It's also likely that if you push sweeping changes without properly getting a lay of the land, you'll step on other people's toes.
This is precisely why we say that one of the most important first things to do as a new PM is to get to know stakeholders and cross-functional teams.
Scheduling meetings and learning first-hand about company norms will show respect for your team.
Even if you can identify major changes to the product or developmental processes, it's best to go slow.
Establish your credibility and reputation as a member of the organization and product manager before making sweeping moves.
Additional Product Manager Interview Resources
If you haven't quite received an interview yet, that's okay! It's well within your reach if you prepare well. If you're looking to get into a product manager career path, be sure to check out some of our interview prep and PM-specific resources.
💬 Review more commonly asked sample PM interview questions.
📖 Read through our company-specific Product Manager interview guides
 Google Product Manager Interview Guide
 Facebook Product Manager Interview Guide

 Amazon Product Manager Interview Guide
👯‍♂️ Practice your behavioral and leadership skills with our mock interview practice tool.
👨‍🎓 Take our complete Product Management interview course.
Get to the next level in your career with Products That Count. Gain access to proven best practices, over 300,000 PMs, PM leaders, a product role only Jobs board, and a top-rated strategic Product Management course.

documents.page_content:
The Product Compass
Product Manager Onboarding: Your First 30/60/90 Days
How to drive maximum impact step-by-step. Notion template with a proven PM onboarding framework. What you should do in your first 30, 60, and 90 days.
Paweł Huryn
Feb 02, 2025
∙ Paid
Hey, Paweł here. Welcome to The Product Compass Newsletter!
Every week, I share actionable insights and resources for PMs. Here’s what you might have recently missed:
 AI Prototyping: The Ultimate Guide For Product Managers
 The Ultimate ChatGPT Prompts Library for Product Managers
 Free AI Agents For Product Managers
 How to Quickly Build SaaS Products With AI (No Coding)
 The Ultimate List of 147 Product Manager Interview Questions
Consider subscribing and upgrading your account for the full experience:
🚀 Announcement: I will fully commit to this community starting March 1
Before we go further, last month, I turned 40. Just a few days earlier, I made a decision: Starting March 1, I’ll fully commit to our community! 😊
It was one of the toughest choices of my career. Ideals is the best product organization I’ve ever been part of. Just like the ones Marty Cagan describes. It has inspired many of my posts.
But balancing writing and a full-time job meant giving up more and more personal time, especially with my kids.
And I couldn't imagine walking away from The Product Compass.
I’m excited to dive into deeper research projects, explore new collaborations, and connect more closely with all of you soon.
P.S. I can’t express how grateful I am to all the paid subscribers who helped make this possible. You’re amazing!
Today, I’m sharing a Product Management onboarding framework I’ve refined over the years and tested across different organizations. It’s designed to help you:
 Maximize your impact as a Product Manager when joining a new company.
 Evaluate the Product Manager onboarding plan in your organization.
Here’s what we’ll cover:
1. Your First 30 Days as a Product Manager: Relationships and Learning
2. Your Days 31-60 as a Product Manager: Deeper Understanding and Quick Wins
3. Your Days 61-90 as a Product Manager: Driving Impact
4. 🔒 [Notion Template]: Product Manager Onboarding: First 30, 60, 90 Days
> You may need to adjust the timeline and details to fit your organization. One of your first tasks is to confirm your onboarding plan with your direct supervisor and set up recurring 1:1 meetings.
P.S. Thank you, Maarten Dalmijn and Siddharth Arora for reviewing this one!
1. Your First 30 Days as a Product Manager: Relationships and Learning
The first 30 days of your Product Manager onboarding are about building relationships, establishing rapport, and learning as much as possible about your product, customers, ways of working, and the strategic context.
The generalized framework that has always worked best for me:
Each item in the Notion collection contains a list of subtasks, for example:
Let’s dive into the details.
Share
1.1 Focus Area: Build Relationships and Establish Rapport
Have 1:1 meetings with the team:
 Your team (Engineers, Designers, QA, PMM)
 Other PMs (ask them to present their areas)
Have 1:1 meetings with key stakeholders:
 Marketing, Sales, Success, Support, Finances, Legal, Head of Product
 Map their power, interest, and communication preferences to engage them properly (see the 2x2 Stakeholders Map Template)
Sync with your direct supervisor:
 Confirm your onboarding plan and performance metrics with your supervisor
 Set recurring 1:1 to discuss your progress, challenges, initiatives, and feedback
> Your attitude: Proactively ask others for feedback and guidance
1.2 Focus Area: Learn the Ways of Working
Learn discovery and delivery practices:
 Product discovery
 Scrum events
 Quality assurance
 Production bugs
 Releases, environments
Request and confirm access to the tools:
 Product analytics (e.g., Amplitude, Clarity)
 Design/collaboration tools (e.g., Figma, Miro, Notion, Jira, Confluence, Slack, Slack channels, and other specific "workspaces" and "wikis")
 Customer feedback channels and account management (e.g., Intercom, CRM)
1.3 Focus Area: Learn Your Product
Learn from the basic materials:
 Review onboarding wikis/videos
 Review internal and public documentation
 If possible, shadow a customer onboarding call
Use your product daily:
 Understand core use cases and user flows;one technique is to help with testing
 Identify at least 3 key insights or potential quick wins, share them with the team
1.4 Focus Area: Understand Your Business
Understand the strategic context:
 Company vision
 Market segments and their Jobs to be Done (aka "personas")
 Value propositions for key segments: JTBD, pain points, features and capabilities, benefits, differentiators
 Key business and customer metrics (e.g., revenue, churn, CSAT, North Star, etc.)
 Identify your team objectives and how they align with product strategy
For highly regulated industries:
 For some industries (finance, healthcare), understanding relevant regulations (GDPR, SOC 2, HIPAA) and how they might affect the product is critical
1.5 Focus Area: Understand Your Customers
Review the existing product artifacts:
 Product Backlog
 Product Requirement Documents (PRDs)
 Recent release notes
 Feature requests
 Product roadmap
 Interview transcripts
 Support chats and calls
 Opportunity Solution Tree for your team
Join early discovery sessions:
 Collaborate with Designers and Engineers to learn about the current opportunities, ideas, and assumptions
Talk to the users:
 Conduct at least 4-8 exploratory customer interviews or visits
 Consider shadowing interviews organized by other PMs
 Document key user needs and pain points
 In B2B, reach out to other decision-makers (buyers)
Track basic users’ activity:
 Start using product analytics
 Review events, session recordings, surveys (e.g., CES or CSAT) to spot basic patterns
2. Your Days 31-60 as a Product Manager: Deeper Understanding and Quick Wins
> Upgrade your account, if you haven't already, to access a dedicated Notion template 👇
In the second 30 days as a new Product Manager, I recommend deepening your understanding of your product area, strategy, and business model.
While you still keep learning, identify the first areas for improvement, work with the team to refine the first ideas (features), and achieve the first quick wins.
The generalized framework that has always worked best for me:
Share
Let’s dive into the details.
2.1 Focus Area: Build Relationships and Establish Rapport
Mid-cycle review with your supervisor:
 Share a self-assessment and key learnings
 Get feedback
 Plan and summarize the next steps
> Your attitude: Talk to the supervisor, peers, and stakeholders and try to identify at least 2 areas for improvement
2.2 Focus Area: Learn the Ways of Working
Learn go-to-market practices:
 How to collaborate with the Product Marketing Manager on GTM
 How to create internal/external documentation
 Are there any differences between major releases, small releases, and hot fixes?
Learn how to reach out to customers:
 How can you publish a survey? Are there any restrictions?
 How can you contact your customers, and where can you find their data? Are there any procedures, e.g., for engaging key accounts?
2.3 Focus Area: Learn Your Product
Understand your product area in depth:
 Develop a deep understanding of your use cases and user flows
 Ensure you understand the top use cases and user flows of other product teams
 Identify dependencies with other product teams
2.4 Focus Area: Understand Your Business
Deepen understanding of the business:
 Revenue streams
 Pricing
 Sales and marketing channels
 Conversion flows and AARRR metrics
 High-level cost structure
Study your key competitors:
 Start using at least 2-3 competitors' products
 Understand their value propositions
 Identify at least 3 key insights
Summarize strategy and business model:
 If this information isn’t well documented, create a short summary of the strategy and business model and share it for feedback. It might be a quick win.
2.5 Focus Area: Understand Your Customers
Participate in Product Discovery:
 Closely collaborate with Designers and Engineers (aka "Product Trio")
 Aim to interview customers weekly to identify new opportunities
 Structure your knowledge (e.g., Opportunity Solution Tree)
 Ideate together, identify hidden assumptions, and test high-risk assumptions
Dive deeper into product analytics:
 Dive deeper into the data to identify at least 3 significant user behavior trends or patterns (e.g., by date, cohort, market segment, or region)
Conduct stakeholder interviews:
 Collect more insights on opportunities and challenges from at least 3 stakeholders
Start refining the Product Backlog:
 Hold at least 2 product backlog refinement sessions (depending on the cadence)
 Involve Engineers early and ask for their feedback
 Start with refining existing product backlog items and defining minor improvements that can become quick wins
3. Your Days 61-90 as a Product Manager: Driving Impact
During your days 61-90 as a new Product Manager, I recommend you become the go-to person in your product area.
You should understand not just customers, products, strategy, and the business model but also competitors, key business metrics, and related trends.
This will enable you to formulate stronger hypotheses, propose better ideas, and communicate like a partner with stakeholders and supervisors.
The generalized framework that has always worked best for me:
Let’s dive into the details.
3.1 Focus Area: Build Relationships and Establish Rapport
90-day review with your supervisor:
 Share a self-assessment and key learnings
 Get feedback
 Plan and summarize the next steps
Your attitude: Try to identify at least 2 areas for improvement (supervisor, peers, stakeholders)
3.2 Focus Area: Learn the Ways of Working
Suggest how to work more effectively:
 Identify at least 1-2 process improvements with expected benefits
 Get feedback from at least 3 team members
3.3 Focus Area: Learn Your Product
Become a go-to product person:
 Become an expert in your product area (features, capabilities, user flows, roadmap)
 Internalize exceptions and special conditions, not just the “happy path” - that’s where you can provide the most value
3.4 Focus Area: Understand Your Business
Deep dive into data analytics:
 Analyze how key business metrics have evolved and how they differ by date, cohort, customer segment, or region
 Identify at least 3 key insights
Shadow sales calls:
 Shadow sales calls to refine your insights further
3.5 Focus Area: Understand Your Customers
Delve into Product Discovery:
 Synthesize insights from user interviews, analytics, product artifacts, and stakeholder feedback
 In B2B, shadow business review calls (Customer Success) for more insight
 Identify, validate, and share at least 3-5 new ideas aligned with your objectives
Suggest future objectives for the team:
 Think of 2-3 possible future objectives for the team
 Explain how they are supported by your research and data, aligned with customer needs, and aligned with strategy
4. [Notion Template] Product Manager Onboarding Plan: First 30, 60, 90 Days
Product Manager Onboarding Framework: first 30, 60, and 90 days
A ready-to-use premium Notion template to duplicate (and many others) is available for premium subscribers to The Product Compass newsletter.
It covers:
 Product Manager first 30 days.
 Product Manager first 60 days.
 Product Manager first 90 days.

metadata.title: 30-60-90 Day Plan For Product Managers + Key Milestones

documents.page_content:
How to Approach Your First 30-60-90 Days as a Product Manager
Every new product manager wants to make their mark, but before you’re viewed as a thought leader with the respect of various internal factions,

Every new product manager wants to make their mark, but before you’re viewed as a thought leader with the respect of various internal factions, you need to build a foundation of respect, collaboration, and trust. These tasks can’t be put off - gaining influence and authority is the core of your first three months on the job. How you spend your first three months in a new product management role sets the tone for your entire tenure with the company.
So what should you do? What should you avoid? What can wait until later? Below are the essential undertakings during the first, second, and third month on the job:
What to do in Your First 30 Days as a Product Manager
There are two traps product managers often fall into when they start a new position. Falling into these can put you on the wrong footing at your new PM job, leaving you to play catch-up later.
Trap One
The first is getting lost in the world of paperwork, documentation and systems. This doesn’t help you really learn how the company works or help you establish your credibility. It can also give folks a negative impression of your engagement and interest, since you’re spending too much time alone at your desk.
Trap Two
The second trap is to “come in hot” and start making proclamations and decisions too quickly.  No one likes a know-it-all, and it doesn’t show much respect for the work people put in before you got there and leads with their own subjective experience bias. That’s why your first 30 days should be non-stop meetings and conversations, with the focus on listening and asking questions.
“Showing up to your new gig and immediately bashing long established norms will backfire,” says Jarie Bolander of The Daily MBA. “Those norms are there for a reason and you must first respect them and then try to figure out how to change it. This is true even if everyone hates the policy or procedure. Go slow and build your reputation up first before challenging company norms.”
The key to avoiding this is to start talking to people, scheduling as many one-on-one meetings as possible with peers, superiors and individual contributors in each part of the organization. The more perspectives you get, the better you can help everyone succeed.
“Try to get time with anyone critical to you performing your job,” says Barron Ernst of Naspers. “You want to spend this time not criticizing people or their jobs, but instead spending the time to understand their role, the metrics they are focusing on, and the problems they are facing. The key here is to listen. If you walk in the door and try to tell a long time employee how to do their job or criticize their thinking, you’re not going to be received well.”
Learn About Priorities, Problems, and People
Now that you’ve shown you’re here to listen and help, these meetings are a chance to discover where you should focus early on for some quick wins, as well as what landmines or simmering problems may be lurking in the shadows.“Identify and surface issues percolating just below the radar that may not have been discussed during the interview process and first few days on the job,” says Vincent Huang of BetterWorks. “A good filter for this was to ask how we could do things faster, more efficiently, or better. This goal of mine included figuring out what the team needed, which was also an opportunity for me to provide additional value."
Of course, the most important meetings are going to be with your new manager, as their perception of you and your work is critical.“Learn about your boss,” says The PM in Heels. “How does she like to assign work? What’s her communication style (email, in person, phone)? What does she expect from you in the first 30-60-90 days?”
If your manager has specific things they’re looking for at these milestones, create a plan and update the status weekly so they can see how you’re progressing.Finally, your first 30 days should include meetings with people outside of your office. Start visiting customers right away so you can avoid your own cognitive biases and get direct product feedback.
“Before any biases set in, let’s them hear and evaluate external feedback. Also arms them with first hand information so they can start contributing when they return to the office,” says Saeed Khan of On Product Management, adding that “As the new PM probably can’t go alone to a lot of the meetings due to lack of product or possibly domain knowledge, provides a good excuse to get one or more other PMs out of the office as well. Helps those PMs traveling together bond as a team which is great for the PM team.”
Get clear on what matters
Turn scattered user data into meaningful customer intelligence, guiding smarter decisions and creating a better product.
Talk to an Expert

documents.page_content:
PM Toolbox
Product Management Basics
Product management basics: your first 30-day plan
By Carla Turner mars 12, 2026 12 min read
Your first 30 days in product can make—or quietly break—your credibility. You’ll walk into meetings where everyone wants “just one more feature,” but nobody can agree on business goals, company goals, or what success even looks like.
This matters more in 2026 because teams are hiring and operating with a sharper focus on immediate revenue and real business outcomes, not speculative bets. If you don’t create a clear understanding of the product manager’s role, decision making defaults to whoever talks loudest, and aligning stakeholders gets harder every week.
This 30-day plan gives you product management basics you can execute: set outcomes on day 1, run discovery that pays off, turn ideas into a plan people trust, ship without chaos, and measure impact. You’ll also see where Product Manager Resources fits when you need practical templates and key product management concepts to keep teams on the same page.
What to do on day 1: set outcomes, not features
Do you want your first week to be a calm alignment exercise or a month of “drive-by” requests and feature debates?
Your fastest path to impact is aligning everyone on a clear user problem, a measurable outcome, and how decision making will happen. This is the product manager’s role at its most practical: turning ambiguity into a clear understanding of what matters, why it matters, and what you’ll do next. When companies in 2026 prioritize immediate revenue over speculative bets, getting to business outcomes early keeps you credible and keeps your team shipping.
Outcomes are easier to set when you can point to a shared destination the team is trying to reach. A crisp product vision gives you that reference point from day one.
Write the problem statement in one paragraph (and what you will not solve)
Write one paragraph that names the user, the pain, the moment it happens, and the current workaround. Add one sentence on what you will not solve so you don’t accidentally accept the whole backlog as “scope.” If you need a starting point, Product Manager Resources templates help you capture the problem, constraints, and product vision without turning it into a mini-novel.
Pick one measurable outcome that defines success
Pick one outcome metric that ties to business goals and can be checked weekly, not quarterly. If your product is self-serve, that might be activation rate or feature adoption; if it’s B2B, it might be time-to-value or expansion revenue impact. A product dashboard should connect what you build to impact, tracking signals like feature adoption and revenue impact in one place.
A plan people trust is as much about alignment as it is about timelines—especially when priorities shift. Use a simple stakeholder management approach to set expectations, handle trade-offs, and keep decision-makers aligned.
Once your plan is clear, the next step is choosing the systems that keep it visible and executable. Here are product management tools that help you turn intent into daily progress.
Once your plan is clear, the next step is running it in a cadence that keeps learning and delivery moving together—see this guide to agile product management.
Map stakeholders and lock decision rules before the first debate
Map stakeholder management fast so aligning stakeholders doesn’t become politics. Put people in one of these buckets so everyone stays on the same page:
 Deciders: who can say yes/no
 Operators: who runs delivery (design/engineering)
 Advisors: who brings evidence (sales, support, data)
 Informed: who needs updates, not meetings
Choose a simple cadence for planning, reviews, and retros
Choose a lightweight cadence you can keep even when things get busy: a weekly planning check, a mid-sprint review for risks, and a retro that ends in one decision. Treat it like product discovery rigor applied to your team’s operating system, and use Product Manager Resources to standardize the artifacts so the conversation stays on company goals, not personal preferences.
Feature-first vs outcome-first: how day-1 framing changes decisions
If you can quantify impact and describe how you level up, you’ll handle a big chunk of what interviews focus on—use these PM interview questions to sharpen your answers.
How to pick the right work: discovery that pays off
Most roadmap churn comes from skipping discovery, not from bad execution. You cut wasted build time when you validate the problem and the value with quick, repeatable habits that keep you close to customer needs and honest about tradeoffs. In a 2026 market that rewards immediate revenue over speculative bets, teams are hiring and funding work that proves impact fast, not work that sounds ambitious.
Discovery is how you identify customer pain you can actually solve, using evidence from user research, market research, and data. Tools and templates from Product Manager Resources help you keep these inputs consistent so insights survive the handoff from conversation to backlog.
If you’re using this plan to break into the role (not just ramp up in it), start with this step-by-step guide to become product manager and set yourself up for a strong first month.
Run user interviews that uncover real constraints, not opinions
Good interviews expose constraints behind customer problems. Ask for recent, specific stories and dig into what blocked them, what they tried, and what “good enough” looked like in the moment. When you prompt for artifacts (screenshots, exports, workarounds), you move from preference to proof and build empathy that design and engineering can act on.
Use product analytics to find the leakiest parts of the journey
Analytics tells you where value fails to show up. Look for drop-offs between key steps, time-to-first-value, and repeated error paths, then pair that with user feedback to understand why the leak happens. A practical product dashboard mindset helps you connect feature adoption and revenue impact to the actual workflow users are stuck in.
Study alternatives and competitors to learn positioning and expectations
Competitive analysis is about expectations, not copying. Map the competitive landscape by comparing what your target market uses today (including spreadsheets and manual processes), then note what customers assume is “standard.” This is where market trends and switching costs reveal real market opportunities.
Turn insights into opportunity statements your team can act on
Opportunity statements translate learning into decisions. Write them in a way that forces clarity: user, context, constraint, and measurable outcome, so engineering can estimate and design can prototype without guessing. If you use Product Manager Resources artifacts for opportunity statements, you’ll keep phrasing consistent enough to compare bets across quarters instead of debating wording every time.
A simple discovery loop that reduces roadmap churn
How to turn ideas into an executable plan people trust
A team I worked with had five “top” ideas after discovery and zero momentum because every meeting restarted the same argument: what are we actually doing first, and why. You earn credibility when you turn those insights into a plan that makes trade offs explicit, aligns to business objectives, and shows how you’ll measure product value.
Write a one-page product brief that clarifies tradeoffs early
A one-page brief is the fastest way to lock product direction before the roadmap debate begins. Keep it scannable: problem, target user, proposed approach, risks, and what you will not do so “nice-to-haves” don’t sneak in later. Product Manager Resources templates work well here because you can reuse the same structure across initiatives and speed up buy in with familiar artifacts.
Prioritize with a consistent framework instead of gut feel
Consistent prioritizing features beats charismatic opinions, especially when the market is pushing for immediate revenue impact and targeted bets in 2026. Pick one framework and stick to it for a quarter so your data driven decisions are comparable across teams. Score work against key metrics and constraints, not excitement.
Build a roadmap that communicates bets, not promises
Your product roadmap should explain what you’re betting on and what you’ll learn, not act like a delivery contract. Use themes tied to outcomes and show confidence levels so stakeholders understand uncertainty without feeling surprised later. A good roadmap makes strategic thinking visible and reduces last-minute escalations.
Define metrics, guardrails, and acceptance criteria before building
Define success before code so the team can execute with a systematic approach instead of shifting goalposts. Align on:
 Metrics tied to the outcome (e.g., activation rate, time-to-value, retention for the target segment)
 Guardrails that must not worsen (e.g., latency, churn in a key cohort, support ticket volume)
 Acceptance criteria that make “done” testable for design, engineering, and QA
This turns your product management process into execution fuel: clearer requirements, faster reviews, and fewer debates disguised as “alignment.”
Turning ideas into a trusted plan using impact vs effort
How to ship with engineering and design without chaos
Run day to day execution like a product release pipeline: lock roles, shorten feedback loops, and force explicit scope tradeoffs before software development starts. When your “done” is testable, the next bottleneck is usually decision latency between designers, engineers, and other product team members.
Clarify roles so decisions don’t ping-pong between functions
Define who decides, who recommends, and who gets consulted so cross functional teams don’t relitigate the same choices in every standup. You’ll ship faster when you work closely with engineering teams on feasibility calls and with designers on interaction tradeoffs, then document the decision rule in one place (even a short note in Jira or Linear). Product Manager Resources is handy here because it gives you lightweight role and decision templates you can reuse across development teams.
Keep execution tight with small slices, fast reviews, and visible risks
Slice product features into thin, shippable increments so software engineering work produces feedback every few days, not every few weeks. Pair that with a review cadence that forces effective communication and makes risk visible early.
 Small slices: ship one workflow step, one permission, or one surface area at a time.
 Fast reviews: schedule design + eng reviews on a fixed rhythm to unblock engineers quickly.
 Visible risks: track top risks (performance, privacy, dependencies) next to the sprint goal.
Manage scope with explicit tradeoffs: time, quality, and learnings
Make tradeoffs explicit by naming what you’re protecting: a date, a quality bar, or a learning goal from an experiment. If you need to cut scope, cut breadth (fewer new features) before you cut coherence (broken flows), and effectively communicate the impact on metrics and support load.
Launch like a pro with rollouts, comms, and support readiness
Plan launches as a rollout, not a moment, using staged exposure and clear comms to reduce surprises. A simple checklist in Product Manager Resources helps you align engineering teams, support, and marketing on what’s changing, what to watch (adoption, errors, satisfaction), and how to respond if something goes sideways.
How to measure impact and level up your PM skills
After the rollout dust settles, do you actually know whether the change drove product success—or just created noise? You become a stronger PM by closing the post-launch feedback loop, tightening how you communicate, and learning from predictable mistakes instead of repeating them.
Run post-launch reviews that lead to decisions, not blame
Make your post-launch review a data driven decision meeting, not a retro therapy session. Pull product analytics that reflect the business and the experience—Monday.com’s dashboard framing is a good prompt: development velocity, feature adoption, customer satisfaction, and revenue impact help you connect shipping to outcomes.
 What happened? Compare expected vs actual on your key metrics (adoption, errors, retention, revenue impact).
 Why did it happen? Use data analysis plus 3–5 real user/support examples to avoid storytelling.
 What will we do next? Decide: iterate, roll back, expand rollout, or re-scope—and assign an owner and date.
Build feedback loops with support, sales, and customer success
Turn frontline signals into a weekly system so you can spot product growth blockers early. Ask for “top 10 tickets by theme,” “top 5 lost deals where product gaps mattered,” and “top 5 renewal risks,” then tie each to an experiment or backlog decision.
Communicate with reusable artifacts that scale your influence
Reuse artifacts so alignment doesn’t depend on meetings. A one-page decision log, a living metrics doc, and a launch/readiness checklist (templates you can keep in Product Manager Resources) let stakeholders self-serve and help you stay up to date on outcomes.
Avoid the most common PM traps that stall careers
The traps are boring—and common—because they look like “being busy.” Watch for these product management fundamentals slipping:
 Confusing output with impact (shipping features without measurable outcomes).
 Tracking vanity metrics instead of key metrics tied to customer satisfaction and revenue.
 Skipping continuous improvement (no post-launch decisions, just more product launches).
 Over-rotating to opinion when product analytics already answers the question.
If you want a simple way to practice these essential skills consistently, keep a lightweight toolkit—your own, or something like Product Manager Resources—to standardize reviews, dashboards, and decision artifacts as your product manager skills grow.
Close the loop: measure impact, learn, and improve
Your first 30 days should create momentum, not noise
Your fastest impact comes from alignment: nail a clear user problem, a measurable outcome, and a decision-making path so stakeholders stop debating and start committing. That shared clarity is the difference between “busy” and “progress” in your first month.
Your credibility comes from disciplined discovery and translation: build quick validation habits, then turn what you learn into crisp requirements, outcome-based prioritization, and a roadmap framed as bets. Tools and templates from Product Manager Resources can help you keep those artifacts consistent and easy to socialize.
Your velocity comes from tight operating rhythms: clarify roles, shorten feedback loops, manage scope deliberately, and close the loop after launch to learn fast. Product Manager Resources is a practical place to standardize your 30-day system.
Next step: open your calendar and schedule five 30-minute user/problem interviews for the next two weeks.

Executive Education 2025 Program Guide At the Kellogg School of Management, our distinctive cross-disciplinary approach to research and teaching drives innovation, creates real-world insights for business leaders and focuses on the issues most critical to contemporary business. It also drives our Executive Education program design, ensuring that you are not only exposed to the most current business thinking, but are ready to immediately and practically apply insights in your organization.
Kellogg is known for a culture that fosters collaboration and challenges leaders to think innovatively. Our programs exemplify that culture by creating an immersive, supportive environment where insights emerge.
Our Executive Education programs are offered in a variety of flexible formats to best meet your learning needs. Whether you choose to enroll in a program in-person or online, you’ll gain the tools to progress your career and organization and to create lasting connections with faculty and peers.
If you are a Kellogg School of Management or Northwestern University alum, enjoy lifetime benefits, including a 15% discount on all Executive Education C-level, year-long online programs and a 30% discount on all other Executive Education open enrollment programs.
Our three unique campuses located in Evanston, Chicago and Miami allow you to benefit from the resources of a major research university and global mindset that instills an expansive view of the world and your organization’s role in it.
Kellogg Executive Education Immersive. Collaborative. Innovative. Practical. 2025 Executive Education Program Guide 3 Open enrollment programs 847.467.6018 ExecEd@kellogg.

Please email or call us if you would like a personal consultation. 4 Kellogg Executive Education The Executive Scholar Certificate A Kellogg credential that is uniquely you As a growth-minded professional, you know that lifelong learning is essential to staying ahead in a rapidly evolving global marketplace. The Executive Scholar Certificate allows you to earn a Kellogg credential that distinguishes you as a leader, is customized to your professional development goals and rewards you with a lifetime of benefits. Become an Executive Scholar by completing any combination of our eligible individual programs totaling 40 or more Scholar Points within four years. Programs are offered in-person, live virtual, and online.
Choose from one of the specializations below or build your own certificate (no specialization) for ultimate flexibility. A specialization, while not required, can be achieved with 20 or more Scholar Points from the same portfolio as listed on our website, with the remaining Scholar Points for the Certificate from any portfolio.
Available specializations: •	Family Enterprises •	Growth & Innovation •	Operations •	Finance •	Leadership •	Strategy •	General Management •	Marketing or Sales •	Nonprofit Management Take the first steps toward your Scholar Certificate Start your journey by submitting an online application and scheduling a consultation with our Executive Scholar Certificate Advisor. To access the application and learn more about the Certificate, visit kell.gg/kxscholars or email scholars@kellogg.northwestern.edu.
	The General Management and Nonprofit Management specializations have separate requirements and may not be combined with other specialization options. Program Scholar Points value and benefits may change at any time without notice. Kellogg Executive Education reserves the right to exclude programs from Scholar Certificate eligibility.
Key benefits Completion of the Kellogg Executive Scholar Certificate comes with exceptional benefits: •	A Certificate of Professional Achievement, including a verifiable digital certificate •	Coaching hours with Kellogg Career & Professional Development Coach •	30% discount on Executive Education open enrollment programs •	15% discount on Executive Education 12-month C-Suite level programs •	10% discount on Executive Education programs for your colleagues (cannot be combined with any other offer or discount) •	Membership in the global Kellogg alumni network and access to the online network •	Opportunities to participate in alumni clubs and select events •	Access to cutting-edge research and content from Kellogg faculty and leading practitioners •	A Kellogg branded email address •	Membership in a private LinkedIn group for Executive Scholars 2025 Executive Education Program Guide 5 General Management Strategic Capabilities for Emerging Business Leaders Building a foundation for growth Live virtual:	December 1–12, 2025 This highly interactive, live virtual program positions the high-performing, emerging leader to make bold career moves, laying the foundation for general management and leadership of the broader business. This six-week program features exercises that allow you to shape your brand strategy and apply the concepts you learn to your own brand portfolio. kell.gg/kxbstrat PG2025–BR 847.467.6018 ExecEd@kellogg.northwestern.edu 2211 Campus Drive | Evanston, IL 60208 kell.gg/kxexeced Northwestern University reserves the right to change without notice any statement in this publication concerning, but not limited to, rules, policies, tuition, fees, curricula and courses. This includes the right to cancel a program at any time for any reason. In case of a cancellation, the university is not responsible for any travel or other related expenses accrued by the program registrant.
Photography: Jason Brown, Nic Lehoux, Heidi Peters, Justin Runquist ©2025 Northwestern University. All rights reserved.
