Social Services | Economic & Family Support Services Day
Pitt County Economic & Family Support Services Day
Career education day scheduled for March 25
PITT COUNTY, N.C. – Pitt County Social Services invites students and those interested in a career in Social Services to an Economic & Family Support Services Day scheduled for Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at Pitt County Human Services located at 403 Government Circle, Greenville.  The event will be held from 9:00 a.m. until 12:30 p.m. with lunch provided.
Programs to be explored include Food and Nutrition Services, Medicaid, Work First and Family Support.  Career positions for these programs are Income Maintenance technicians and caseworkers, Child Support agents, Staff Development specialists and office and administrator positions.
Insight and education on navigating paths toward a career in Social Services include Pitt Community College continuing education courses, NCFAST, and WorkKeys/National Career Readiness Certificate.
The deadline to register is Friday, March 6, 2026.
For more information and registration, contact Thomas Knight at 252-902-1238 or Thomas.Knight@PittCountyNC.gov or Taylor Fedash at 252-902-1140or Taylor.Fedash@PittCountyNC.gov.

Awareness | Severe Weather Preparedness Week
Pitt County Recognizes Severe Weather Preparedness Week
Awareness activities planned for the first week in March
PITT COUNTY, N.C. – Severe Weather Preparedness Week runs Sunday, March 1 through Saturday, March 7, 2026. To raise awareness, Pitt County Emergency Management (PCEM) and Pitt County Schools (PCS) have scheduled activities throughout the week.
Tornado Drill: On Wednesday, March 4, 2026 at 9:30 a.m., the National Weather Service in cooperation with local broadcasters will conduct a statewide tornado drill.  Emergency Management Director Randy Gentry will join Pitt County Schools officials at Ayden Elementary School for this drill.  Ayden Elementary School is located at 187 Third Street, Ayden, NC 28513.
Media Advisory:  Officials will be available to the media from 9:00 a.m. to 9:20 a.m.
The Public Information Office will feature important safety related posts, related to the daily theme, on its Facebook pages @PittCountyNC and website www.PittCountyNC.gov.
“Topics for this year include safety information related to severe thunderstorms and tornados, how to receive severe weather alerts, high wind and hail events, lightening safety and flash flood safety,” says Randy Gentry, PCEM Director.
PCEM utilizes Hyper-Reach, a state-of-the-art emergency alert system designed specifically for public safety.  It provides rapid notification of hazardous and urgent situations using a mix of telephone calls, text and email messages, and even TTY/TDD service for the hearing impaired.
Community members and people who work in Pitt County are encouraged to enroll by calling or texting “Alert” to 252-347-0170 or online 
 To learn more, visit www.PittCountyNC.gov/SevereWeather or call PCEM at 252-902-3950.
Pitt County’s Mission is to enhance the community’s well-being with care, respect and efficiency.  Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov
Media Contact:  Dawn C. Jones, Director, Public Information & Media Relations | dawn.jones@pittcountync.gov | (252) 902-2993

Tornado Drill: On Wednesday, March 4, 2026 at 9:30 a.m., the National Weather Service in cooperation with local broadcasters will conduct a statewide tornado drill.  Emergency Management Director Randy Gentry will join Pitt County Schools officials at Ayden Elementary School for this drill.  Ayden Elementary School is located at 187 Third Street, Ayden, NC 28513.
Media Advisory:  Officials will be available to the media from 9:00 a.m. to 9:20 a.m.
The Public Information Office will feature important safety related posts, related to the daily theme, on its Facebook pages @PittCountyNC and website www.PittCountyNC.gov.
“Topics for this year include safety information related to severe thunderstorms and tornados, how to receive severe weather alerts, high wind and hail events, lightening safety and flash flood safety,” says Randy Gentry, PCEM Director.
PCEM utilizes Hyper-Reach, a state-of-the-art emergency alert system designed specifically for public safety.  It provides rapid notification of hazardous and urgent situations using a mix of telephone calls, text and email messages, and even TTY/TDD service for the hearing impaired.
Community members and people who work in Pitt County are encouraged to enroll by calling or texting “Alert” to 252-347-0170 or online 
 To learn more, visit www.PittCountyNC.gov/SevereWeather or call PCEM at 252-902-3950.
Pitt County’s Mission is to enhance the community’s well-being with care, respect and efficiency.  Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov
Media Contact:  Dawn C. Jones, Director, Public Information & Media Relations | dawn.jones@pittcountync.gov | (252) 902-2993

Social Work | National Awareness Month
Pitt County Recognizes National Social Work Month
This year’s theme is "Uplift, Defend, Transform"
PITT COUNTY, N.C. – March is recognized as National Social Work Month, offering an opportunity to spotlight the social work profession and honor the remarkable contributions made by Pitt County social workers.
The “Uplift, Defend, Transform” campaign for Social Work Month provides an opportunity to raise awareness among the public, legislators, and employers about the essential work social workers do in supporting individuals daily, helping them navigate life’s challenges and reach their fullest potential.
“I would like to take a moment to recognize the unwavering dedication and hard work of our DSS staff, who consistently serve the residents of our community and embody the County’s mission to enhance the community’s well-being with care, respect and efficiency,” says Janis Gallagher, County Manager.
Agency Accomplishments From The Last 12 Months
 Adoption of 13 children and permanence for 100 children
 Child protection interventions involving 1,230 families
 Child protective in-home services to 174 families
 Guardianship service for 207 adults
 Protection intervention to more than 320 vulnerable seniors and disabled adults
 More than 1,567 child care subsidy cases
 Utility assistance to 6,969 households
 Transition of 125 individuals toward sustained employment through the Employment and Training program
“We are proud to celebrate National Social Work Month and recognize the steadfast dedication and compassion of our staff in making a lasting impact on the lives of the individuals and families we serve,” says Sharon Rochelle, Director of Pitt County Social Services.
For more information, contact the Public Information Office at 252-902-2995 or  Visit

Public Hearing Notices | Zoning Map Amendments
NOTICE OF PUBLIC HEARINGS
Pitt County Zoning Map Amendments
The Pitt County Board of Commissioners will hold public hearings on Monday, March 16, 2026, at or after 6:00 p.m. in the Eugene James Auditorium in the County Administration Building located at 1717 W. 5th Street, Greenville, N.C. to consider the following requests:
•    Rezoning request byMichael Hammond to rezone 15.18 acres of property owned by Frank Dail Farms, Inc. from Rural Agricultural (RA) to General Commercial (Conditional District) (GC(CD)) to operate a wholesale farm equipment and hardware supply business.  The subject property is identified by the Tax Assessor’s Office as Parcel Number 13255, and is located on the western side of SR 1139 (Moye-Turnage Road), north of its intersection with US 264 (East Marlboro Road) in the Farmville Township
•    Rezoning request by Stonewood Solar, LLC to rezone 235.9 acres of property owned by OM, LLC from Rural Agricultural (RA) to Rural Agricultural (Conditional District) (RA(CD)) to operate a Solare Energy Facility (i.e. “solar farm”).  The subject property is identified by the Tax Assessor’s Office as Parcel Number 58349, and is located on the northwestern corner at the intersection of NC 903 North and SR 1517 (Oakley Road); and the southern side of SR 1521 (Sweet Gum Grove Church Road) in the Carolina Township.
The purpose of these public hearings is to gather public comment and input on the proposed requests prior to consideration by the Board of County Commissioners.  Citizens interested in obtaining additional information on these requests should contact the Pitt County Planning Department at (252) 902-3250.  Those persons unable to attend the hearing may send written questions or comments via email to the Clerk to the Board at Kimberly.Hines@pittcountync.gov or by mailing to: Clerk to the Board, Pitt County, 1717 W. 5th Street, Greenville, N.C. 27834.  All meetings can be viewed by the public on PittTV (Optimum TV Channel 13) or the Pitt County YouTube channel (www.YouTube.com/PittCountyNC)).

Community Outreach | March for Meals
Join Us For The 2026 March for Meals
For more than 20 years, the nationwide Meals on Wheels network has claimed the month of March as a time to unite, strengthen its impact and rally communities around the vital work of serving older adults.
Why the Month of March?The annual March for Meals celebration commemorates March 22, 1972, when President Richard Nixon signed into law an amendment to the Older Americans Act of 1965 establishing a national nutrition program for seniors age 60 and older.
Since 2002, local programs across the country have celebrated March to raise awareness and secure the support needed to meet the growing demand for services. Today, Meals on Wheels programs continue to address food insecurity and malnutrition, combat social isolation, promote independence and improve the health and well-being of older adults in our communities.
What’s Involved?March for Meals is a grassroots movement, allowing local programs to tailor activities to best serve their communities. Campaign efforts typically include:Fundraising Activities – Hosted independently or in partnership with local businessesVolunteer Drives – Engaging community members and local organizationsCommunity Champions Activities – Public appearances, meal preparation and/or deliveries by elected officials, local celebrities, business leaders and members of the mediaCommunity Outreach – Connecting with supporters and increasing awareness through local media and communications efforts
Community Champions WeekMarch 16–20, 2026
During Community Champions Week, we encourage local, state and federal officials, along with other prominent community figures, to help deliver meals, speak out for seniors and raise awareness about the importance of Meals on Wheels.
This special week provides an excellent opportunity to spotlight the nutritional and social needs of seniors, generate media attention and strengthen community support.
Help us make March 2026 a month of action, awareness and advocacy as we celebrate the compassionate work of Meals on Wheels programs and the difference they make in seniors’ lives. Visit the official Pitt County event page for details and resources at  or click here to sign up.

Emergency Management | Hazmat Event
Emergency Management Responds to Local Hazmat Event
PITT COUNTY, N.C. – Pitt County EMS was dispatched to Boviet Solar located at 1125 Sugg Pkwy for two persons reported unconscious at 9:30 a.m. on Wednesday, February 25, 2026. Upon arrival, treatment began and transport of both patients to ECU Health was initiated. The CO monitors carried by EMS crews did not alert to hazardous air and no obvious signs of hazardous conditions were observed.
At approximately 10:25 a.m., the 911 Communications Center notified the County EMS Supervisor that multiple additional persons were requesting EMS from the facility. Pitt County Emergency Management (PCEM) Deputy Director/Fire Marshal responded to find a building evacuation in progress and multiple people in their personal vehicles feeling unwell.
When it became apparent that more than 4-6 persons were requesting to be evaluated, a request was made to ECU Health SMAT to send the Medical Ambulance Bus. Additionally, two EMS physicians were dispatched by the 911 Communications Center. EMS personnel prioritized patients needing transport and levied available resources to manage patient care.
Incident Command and Fire Departments (Staton House, Pactolus, Bethel and Stokes) conducted employee search and air monitoring operations in the facility to determine a source or cause of the issue.
During the evacuation, EMS evaluated 28 patients with 18 of those requiring/requesting transport. This includes the initial two persons encountered in the first 911 call. All patients were transported to ECU Health Medical Center.
There were approximately 200 employees or others on site at time of the evacuation.
NC Regional Response Team arrived from Williamston, N.C. along with NC Emergency Management representatives. The investigation found environmental factors in the industrial setting that could contribute to the incident but no source of carbon monoxide or hazardous gases was located. Prior to the hazmat units clearing the scene, personnel conducted air monitoring in the facility with facility staff and all machinery operational to ensure there was no hazard present. No hazards were indicated at that time. The facility was deemed safe to operate, but the incident remains under investigation. At approximately 5:30 p.m. on Wednesday, emergency response resources had cleared the scene and returned facility operations to Boviet Solar staff.

Half-Price Adoption Special | Animal Services
Community Sponsored Half-Price Adoption Special Begins February 25
Reduced fees for Animal Services eligible dogs and cats over one yearPitt County Animal Services (PCAS) announces a community sponsored adoption special beginning Wednesday, February 25, 2026 for all dogs and cats over one year of age at its Shelter located at 4550 County Home Road, Greenville, N.C.
Through partially funded community sponsorships, adoption fees for eligible animals will be reduced to half price.  The half price adoption fee will remain in effect until each animal is adopted.
“The Animal Shelter is at capacity due to high intake numbers. During the week of February 16th, 16 dogs were taken in on one day. As of Friday, February 20, the Shelter is caring for 86 dogs and 34 cats, with additional animals expected to arrive,” says Ashley Case, Animal Services Volunteer Coordinator.
Through this special, half-price adoption fees for eligible animals over one year will be $48.00 for cats and $68.00 for dogs.  Fees include spay/neuter, age appropriate vaccinations, microchipping, heartworm testing for dogs and deworming.
“Pitt County Animal Services is very fortunate to be supported by a caring community network.  This special is made possible through sponsored adoption fees provided by community members, volunteers and supporters who wish to help animals find homes. Traditionally, these sponsorships are not publicly advertised and are often a surprise for adopters, with many choosing to “pay it forward” by helping sponsor another animal. For this initiative, available sponsorships are being applied to reduce adoption fees and increase placement opportunities for adult animals who are often overlooked,” says Kimmie Mitchell, Animal Services Director.
All adoptions require an approved application. Once an application is submitted, Shelter staff will place an “adoption pending” hold on the animal and contact the adopter within 72 hours to continue the adoption process.  Operating hours for adoptions are 8:30 a.m. – 4:30 p.m. Monday – Friday.  Appointments are preferred, but not necessary.
“The objective of this effort is to help adult animals find homes while easing Shelter capacity and encouraging continued community support through sponsorships,” continues Mitchell.
For more information on eligible animals, contact Animal Servicesat252-902-1725, option 1 or Ashley.Case@pittcountync.gov.
Media inquiries may be submitted to the Public Information office at 252-902-2993 or Dawn.Jones@PittCountyNC.gov.
Pitt County’s Mission is to enhance the community’s well-being with care, respect and efficiency.  Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov

All adoptions require an approved application. Once an application is submitted, Shelter staff will place an “adoption pending” hold on the animal and contact the adopter within 72 hours to continue the adoption process.  Operating hours for adoptions are 8:30 a.m. – 4:30 p.m. Monday – Friday.  Appointments are preferred, but not necessary.
“The objective of this effort is to help adult animals find homes while easing Shelter capacity and encouraging continued community support through sponsorships,” continues Mitchell.
For more information on eligible animals, contact Animal Servicesat252-902-1725, option 1 or Ashley.Case@pittcountync.gov.
Media inquiries may be submitted to the Public Information office at 252-902-2993 or Dawn.Jones@PittCountyNC.gov.
Pitt County’s Mission is to enhance the community’s well-being with care, respect and efficiency.  Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov
Community Sponsored Half-Price Adoption Special Begins February 25
Reduced fees for Animal Services eligible dogs and cats over one yearPitt County Animal Services (PCAS) announces a community sponsored adoption special beginning Wednesday, February 25, 2026 for all dogs and cats over one year of age at its Shelter located at 4550 County Home Road, Greenville, N.C.
Through partially funded community sponsorships, adoption fees for eligible animals will be reduced to half price.  The half price adoption fee will remain in effect until each animal is adopted.
“The Animal Shelter is at capacity due to high intake numbers. During the week of February 16th, 16 dogs were taken in on one day. As of Friday, February 20, the Shelter is caring for 86 dogs and 34 cats, with additional animals expected to arrive,” says Ashley Case, Animal Services Volunteer Coordinator.
Through this special, half-price adoption fees for eligible animals over one year will be $48.00 for cats and $68.00 for dogs.  Fees include spay/neuter, age appropriate vaccinations, microchipping, heartworm testing for dogs and deworming.
“Pitt County Animal Services is very fortunate to be supported by a caring community network.  This special is made possible through sponsored adoption fees provided by community members, volunteers and supporters who wish to help animals find homes. Traditionally, these sponsorships are not publicly advertised and are often a surprise for adopters, with many choosing to “pay it forward” by helping sponsor another animal. For this initiative, available sponsorships are being applied to reduce adoption fees and increase placement opportunities for adult animals who are often overlooked,” says Kimmie Mitchell, Animal Services Director.
All adoptions require an approved application. Operating hours for adoptions are 8:30 a.m. – 4:30 p.m. Monday – Friday.  Appointments are preferred, but not necessary.

Government Communications | Awareness Day
Recognizing the Public Servants Supporting Effective Government Communications
Government Communicators Awareness Day recognizes the professionals who help connect government and the community through clear, reliable, and timely information. Observed each year on the third Friday in February, the day highlights behind-the-scenes work that supports transparency, accountability, and public understanding.
Throughout the year, government communicators help keep residents, businesses, and visitors informed by explaining how local government works, sharing important updates, and guiding people to programs, services, and resources.
Serving Pitt County, the Public Information Office leads these efforts by connecting departments with the community and sharing accurate, accessible information across multiple platforms. These efforts include local news media, social media, the county website, PittTV, and other outreach channels that help residents stay informed and engaged.

Notice of Advertisement | Upset Bid For Sale of Real Property
Notice of Advertisement for Upset Bid For Sale of Real Property
Pitt County is in receipt of an offer to purchase county owned property in the amount of $7,900 by Terry Williams.  The subject property is identified by the Tax Assessor's Office as Parcel 11346. The subject property is .09 acres located in Farmville, Pitt County, North Carolina, that was acquired by Pitt County in 2008 through tax foreclosure.
In accordance with North Carolina General Statute 160A-269, please take notice that within 10 days any person may raise either bid by not less than ten percent (10%) of the first one thousand dollars ($1,000) and five percent (5%) of the remainder.  When a bid is raised, the bidder shall deposit with the Clerk to the Board of County Commissioners five percent (5%) of the increased bid, and the Clerk shall re-advertise the offer at the increased bid.  This procedure shall be repeated until no further qualifying upset bids are received, at which time the Board of Commissioners may accept the offer and sell the property to the highest bidder.  The Board reserves the right to reject any and all offers.  Citizens interested in obtaining additional information on the properties or placing an upset bid may contact the Pitt County Manager's Office at (252) 902-2950.  This the 14th, day of February, 2026.

Human Relations Commission | Black History Month Event
Pitt County Human Relations Commission to Host Black History Month Event
Pitt County Human Relations Commission (HRC) announces aBlack History Month event recognizing the landmark court case resulting in integration of Pitt County public schools at its regular meeting scheduled for Tuesday, February 17, 2026 at 6:00 p.m. in the Eugene James Auditorium of the County Administration Building located at 1717 W 5th Street, Greenville.
The purpose of the event is to recognize, by proclamation, the history of Pitt County public schools’ desegregation. The United States Supreme Court ruled unanimously on May 17, 1954 in Brown v Board of Education (1954) that segregation in American Public Schools on the basis of race violated the Fourteenth Amendment. In 1956, the State of North Carolina enacted the Pearsall Plan, which delegated desegregation related issues to local school districts.
Pitt County public schools remained segregated until 1965, when Teel, et al v Pitt County Board of Education (1965) was heard in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of North Carolina.
In August 1965, more than 200 African-American students began attending school with white students throughout Pitt County.
“The Commission wishes to recognize the bravery and sacrifice of the families who participated in the integration of Pitt County Schools. The public is invited to attend this Black History Month event,” says Kimberly Moody, Chair, Pitt County Human Relations Commission.
For more information,contact Kimberly MoodyCall 252-902-2959 or Email Kimberly.Moody@PittCountyNC.gov

Community Invited | Diabetes Support Group Schedule
Pitt County Announces 2026 Diabetes Support Group Schedule
Free monthly program offered to residents living with diabetes
PITT COUNTY, N.C. – Pitt County Public Health invites community members living with diabetes, as well as their caregivers and supporters, to participate in its 2026 Diabetes Support Group. The monthly program is designed to provide education, encouragement and practical tools for managing diabetes and improving overall well-being.
The Diabetes Support Group meets on thesecond Wednesday of each month from 10:00 a.m. to 11:30 a.m. at the Pitt County Health Department located at 201 Government Circle, Greenville, N.C.
“Participants can expect to hear from inspiring experts and guest speakers, engage in practical topics and interactive activities, learn healthy recipes and connect with others who understand the diabetes journey,” says Wes Gray, Public Health Director.
“Registration is not required and the program is free and open to the public. The group emphasizes skill-building, shared experiences and supportive discussion in a welcoming environment,” adds Patti Columbe, Diabetes Community Care Coordinator.
The 2026 meeting topics include:
 ### January 14: Ring in the New Year Together: Goals for a Healthier You!
 February 11: Arm-ed and Ready: Immunizations (Guest Speaker)
 March 11: Food Label Finesse: Decoding Nutrition for Better Blood Sugar
 April 8: Motivation Station (Guest Speaker)
 May 13: Spring into Fitness: Enjoyable Movement and Exercise
 June 10: The Sweet Spot: Managing Diabetes at Picnics, BBQs, and Gatherings
 July 8: Seeds of Happiness: Gardening (Guest Speaker)
 August 12: Skin and Sole Survivors: Essential Foot and Skin Care
 September 9: Kidney Care: Preventing Diabetic Nephropathy (Guest Speaker)
 October 14: Diabetes Bingo & Success Stories: Sharing Tips and Triumphs
 November 10: Special Event: Living Well with Diabetes
 December 9: Holiday Health Hacks: Enjoying Celebrations Without Blood Sugar Stress
Pitt County Public Health encourages individuals at all stages of diabetes management to attend and take advantage of the opportunity to learn, share, and build supportive connections.
For more information, contact Patti Columbe at 252-902-2332 or patti.columbe@pittcountync.gov.
The Mission of Pitt County Government is to enhance the health, safety, and well-being of our community by advocating for and providing quality services in a friendly and cost-effective manner. Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov
Media Contact: Dawn C. Jones, Director, Public Information & Media Relations | dawn.jones@pittcountync.gov | (252) 902-2993

Animal Services | Sweetheart Foster Weekend
Pitt County Animal Services to Begin “Sweetheart Foster Weekend”
Foster program sends animals to loving homes for Valentine’s Day
PITT COUNTY, N.C. – Pitt County Animal Services (PCAS) announces its holiday foster program “Sweetheart Foster Weekend” will begin Friday, February 13, 2026.  The program is a short term foster opportunity designed to get dogs, cats, kittens and puppies out of the shelter and into a home during the holiday.
PCAS will supply food, bowls, toys and blankets for all foster pets. Foster dogs and puppies will come with a leash and crate while cats and kittens will have a collar and carrier.  Animal Services provides the supplies free of charge.
“‘Sweetheart Foster Weekend’ is a great opportunity for anyone curious about fostering but not ready for a long-term commitment. The holiday is about celebrating love and we invite residents to open their homes and offer animals a cozy place to spend the weekend,” says Kimberly Mitchell, Director of Animal Services.
As of Friday, January 30, there are 71 dogs and puppies and 25 cats and kittens at the Animal Shelter located at 4550 County Home Road, Greenville.
Foster parents can schedule a pick up and have the option to return or adopt following the schedule below:
Pick UpFriday, February 13, 20262:00 p.m. – 4:00 p.m.
Drop Off/AdoptionMonday, February 16, 20269:00 a.m. – 11:00 a.m.
For more information about fostering, visit www.PittCountyNC.gov/PCAS or contact Ashley Case at (252) 902-1725 Opt. 1 or Ashley.Case@PittCountyNC.gov.
Media inquiries may be requested to Dawn Jones at(252) 270-8035 or PIO.Mgmt@PittCountyNC.gov.
The Mission of Pitt County Government is to enhance the health, safety, and well-being of our community by advocating for and providing quality services in a friendly and cost-effective manner. Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov

The program is a short term foster opportunity designed to get dogs, cats, kittens and puppies out of the shelter and into a home during the holiday.
PCAS will supply food, bowls, toys and blankets for all foster pets. Foster dogs and puppies will come with a leash and crate while cats and kittens will have a collar and carrier.  Animal Services provides the supplies free of charge.
“‘Sweetheart Foster Weekend’ is a great opportunity for anyone curious about fostering but not ready for a long-term commitment. The holiday is about celebrating love and we invite residents to open their homes and offer animals a cozy place to spend the weekend,” says Kimberly Mitchell, Director of Animal Services.
As of Friday, January 30, there are 71 dogs and puppies and 25 cats and kittens at the Animal Shelter located at 4550 County Home Road, Greenville.
Foster parents can schedule a pick up and have the option to return or adopt following the schedule below:
Pick UpFriday, February 13, 20262:00 p.m. – 4:00 p.m.
Drop Off/AdoptionMonday, February 16, 20269:00 a.m. – 11:00 a.m.
For more information about fostering, visit www.PittCountyNC.gov/PCAS or contact Ashley Case at (252) 902-1725 Opt. 1 or Ashley.Case@PittCountyNC.gov.
Media inquiries may be requested to Dawn Jones at(252) 270-8035 or PIO.Mgmt@PittCountyNC.gov.
The Mission of Pitt County Government is to enhance the health, safety, and well-being of our community by advocating for and providing quality services in a friendly and cost-effective manner. Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov
Media Contact: Dawn C. Jones, Director, Public Information & Media Relations | dawn.jones@pittcountync.gov | (252) 902-2993

EMS | Enhancing Patient Care
Pitt County EMS Enhances Patient Care with New Mobile Technology
Sapphire IV Infusion Pumps installed on County EMS fleet
Pitt County Emergency Medical Services (EMS) has enhanced patient care capabilities by equipping County ambulances and Quick Response Vehicles (QRVs) with Sapphire IV infusion pumps, providing safer, more precise medication and fluid delivery during emergency responses and patient transport.
The Sapphire IV infusion pumps allow emergency responders to administer medications and IV fluids with greater accuracy and consistency, helping reduce the risk of dosing errors while improving patient outcomes. The compact, advanced technology is designed specifically for mobile and pre-hospital environments.
“Administering medication to pediatric patients is more complex than with adult patients,” says Jim McArthur, Emergency Management Deputy Director/EMS Coordinator.  “These pumps are a significant advancement and give our paramedics advanced tools to deliver critical medications more precisely to patients of all ages, especially during high-acuity calls where accuracy and efficiency are essential.”
The new infusion pumps will support a wide range of treatments, including cardiac medications, pain management, and critical care interventions. By standardizing this equipment across the ambulance and QRV fleet, Pitt County EMS ensures consistent care regardless of which unit responds.
“Our paramedics have completed training on the Sapphire IV infusion pumps, online modules, classroom instruction, and simulation scenarios, and are fully prepared to bring them into patient care,” said Jonathan Croom, Pitt County EMS Training Chief. “Fleet-wide rollout begins in February, ensuring safe, effective use from day one.”
Download equipment and training images.
For more information, contact Pitt County Emergency Management at 252-902-3950.
The Mission of Pitt County Government is to enhance the health, safety, and well-being of our community by advocating for and providing quality services in a friendly and cost-effective manner. Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov

February 4, 2026 | Services/Programs Closure Update
Pitt County Government Offices, Services/Programs Closure Update
Schedule changes happening February 4
Pitt County Government offices, including Public Health clinical services, will be closed on Wednesday, February 4, 2026.
“I extend my appreciation and gratitude for the significant number of dedicated County employees who have continued to work throughout this challenging weather event. Due to the condition of secondary roads, continuing precipitation and cold temperature forecasted, and out of an abundance of caution for the safety of staff and those we serve, County offices will be closed tomorrow, says Janis Gallagher, County Manager.
Cultural Arts & Recreation programs/classes are cancelled for Wednesday, February 4.
Pitt Area Transit System (PATS) services will be limited on Wednesday, February 4.
Patients with clinic and PATS appointments will be contacted for rescheduling.
Solid Waste and Recycling staff will continue to clear convenience sites as conditions allow. An update on reopening will be released when known. The Transfer Station, located at 3025 Landfill Road, Greenville is open.
The Animal Services Advisory Board meeting scheduled for 5:30 p.m. on Wednesday, February 4 at the Cultural Arts & Recreation Building located at 4561 County Home Road, Greenville has been cancelled.
Full closure information related to this weather event can be found on the Pitt County Government homepage and closings/delays pagewww.PittCountyNC.gov/Closings, or social media account:  Facebook @PittCountyNC.
For more information, please contact the Public Information Office at252-902-8035.
The Mission of Pitt County Government is to enhance the health, safety, and well-being of our community by advocating for and providing quality services in a friendly and cost-effective manner. Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov

Pitt County Recognizes American Heart Month
Pitt County Recognizes American Heart Month
Special programming and education efforts planned during February
PITT COUNTY, N.C. – In recognition of February as American Heart Month, Pitt County Government is urging employees and community members to take charge of their cardiovascular health by understanding risks and adopting healthier lifestyles.  The following is planned throughout the month.
Awareness for AllNational Wear Red Day is Friday, February 6, 2026.  Cardiovascular Disease is the number one killer of women.  Since 2004, Go Red for Women, the American Heart Association’s signature initiative for women, has focused on improving awareness and care for women affected by cardiovascular disease (CVD).  In support of Go Red Day, County employees and the general public are invited to wear red.  The cupula of the Pitt County Courthouse, located at 100 W 3rd Street Greenville, will be lit red on February 6.  The Public Information Office will promote heath tips from the American Heart Association on its Facebook page @PittCountyNC all month.  The public is encouraged to follow to learn more.
Employee EducationA virtual Hands-Only CPR Class sponsored by Triangle/ ENC American Heart Association will be offered to Pitt County employees on Wednesday, February 4, 2026 from 11:30 a.m.-12:30 p.m. County employees will have an opportunity to increase their knowledge and awareness about Hands-Only CPR and learn how to respond in the event of an emergency.  Employees interested in registering may email nichelle.shuck@pittcountync.gov by Tuesday, February 3, 2026.
Pitt County EMS will offer two in-person CPR classes for County employees on Wednesday, February 25, 2026 at the Pitt County Office Park Community Center.  The first session will be held from 8:30- 10:00 a.m. and the second from3:30-5:00 p.m.  Participants will earn one Wellness Point and may sign up at.
“Heart disease impacts families across our community, but many risk factors are preventable,” says Janis Gallagher, Pitt County Manager.  “American Heart Month is an opportunity to remind our employees and community members that small, everyday choices, like staying active, eating well and learning life-saving skills such as CPR, can make a lasting difference in heart health.”
Join!Cultural Arts & Recreation will host a community Aerobic Soul Line Dance Social on Friday, February 13 from 6:00 p.m.- 8:00 p.m. at the Pitt County Office Park Community Center located at 415 Government Circle, Greenville, NC 27834.  Community members can step, slide and shuffle the night away!  Whether you're an experienced dancer or lacing up your dancing shoes for the first time, this is the perfect opportunity to move, meet new friends and enjoy a lively atmosphere. Our experienced and friendly instructor and fellow dancers will be on hand to help participants learn the steps.
“We invite our community to visit our new Community Center at the Pitt County Office Park complex!  There’s no need to be shy, everyone is welcome on the dance floor. Light refreshments are included, comfortable shoes are recommended and good vibes required!  Register today atPittCountyNC.gov/Recreation,” continues Gallagher.
To learn more about American Heart Month and resources for healthy living, visit heart.org
For more information, contact the Public Information Office at 252-902-2955.

Public Hearing Notice | Zoning Map Amendment
NOTICE OF PUBLIC HEARING
Pitt County Zoning Map Amendment
The Pitt County Board of Commissioners will hold a public hearing on Monday, February 16, 2026, at or after 6:00 pm in the Eugene James Auditorium in the County Administration Building located at 1717 W. 5th Street, Greenville, N.C. to consider the following requests:
•    Rezoning request by Christopher E. Sutton to rezone 45.62 acres of property owned by Stokes Sutton Farms, LLC from Rural Agricultural (RA) to Rural Residential (RR).  The subject property is identified by the Tax Assessor’s Office as Parcel Number 21731, and is located at the southeastern corner of the intersection of SR 1725 (County Home Road) and SR 2241 (Ivy Road) in the Swift Creek Township.
The purpose of this public hearing is to gather public comment and input on the proposed request prior to consideration by the Board of County Commissioners.  Citizens interested in obtaining additional information on this request should contact the Pitt County Planning Department at (252) 902-3250.  Those persons unable to attend the hearing may send written questions or comments via email to the Clerk to the Board at Kimberly.Hines@pittcountync.gov or by mailing to: Clerk to the Board, Pitt County, 1717 W. 5th Street, Greenville, N.C. 27834.  All meetings can be viewed by the public on PittTV (Optimum TV Channel 13) or the Pitt County YouTube channel (www.YouTube.com/PittCountyNC)).

Pitt County Government Declares State of Emergency/Closed Monday
Schedule changes due to forecasted winter weather
Pitt County Government will issue a declaration of state of emergency effective 5:00 p.m. on Friday, January 30, 2026 due to forecasted inclement weather.  Emergency Management is prepared to open the Emergency Operations Center if needed.  Government offices, including Public Health clinical services, will beclosed on Monday, February 2, 2026.
The February 2 Capital Improvement Budget 3:00 p.m. meeting and the 6:00 p.m. regular meeting of the Board of County Commissioners is cancelled.  A public meeting notice will be released when rescheduled.
Cultural Arts & Recreation will close all facilities on Saturday, January 31 and Monday, February 2.
Pitt Area Transit System (PATS) will operate normal hours on Saturday, January 31, weather permitting.
Solid Waste and Recycling (Transfer Station and Convenience Sites) will operate on its normal schedule.

Emergency Management is prepared to open the Emergency Operations Center if needed.  Government offices, including Public Health clinical services, will beclosed on Monday, February 2, 2026.
The February 2 Capital Improvement Budget 3:00 p.m. meeting and the 6:00 p.m. regular meeting of the Board of County Commissioners is cancelled.  A public meeting notice will be released when rescheduled.
Cultural Arts & Recreation will close all facilities on Saturday, January 31 and Monday, February 2.
Pitt Area Transit System (PATS) will operate normal hours on Saturday, January 31, weather permitting.
Solid Waste and Recycling (Transfer Station and Convenience Sites) will operate on its normal schedule.
Full closure information related to this weather event can be found on the Pitt County Government homepage and closings/delays pagewww.PittCountyNC.gov/Closings, or social media account:  Facebook @PittCountyNC.
For more information, please contact the Public Information Office at252-902-2955.
The Mission of Pitt County Government is to enhance the health, safety, and well-being of our community by advocating for and providing quality services in a friendly and cost-effective manner. Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov

2026 Greenville-Pitt County Senior Games Registration
2026 Greenville-Pitt County Senior Games Registration
Celebrating 43 years of fun and fitness for seniors in Pitt County
PITT COUNTY, N.C. – Pitt County Cultural Arts & Recreation announces registration for the 43rd Annual Greenville-Pitt County Senior Games is now open.  The Local Games are scheduled for April 6 - April 25, 2026, with the deadline for registration set for Thursday, March 5, 2026.
 Athletes and artists age 50 and over will have an opportunity to compete in more than 40 sports events, and SilverArts (including visual, literary, performing, and heritage). Sports events include pickleball, horseshoes, walking, swimming, basketball, softball, bicycling, bowling, billiards, table tennis, team sports for softball and 3-on-3 basketball, and more.  Explore the categories list at PittCountyNC.gov/SeniorGames
“The Senior Games are about more than competition; they’re about connection, wellness and celebrating the talents of adults 50 and better,” says Jennifer Lanier-Coward, Director, Pitt County Cultural Arts & Recreation. “We’re proud to offer inclusive opportunities that keep our senior community active, engaged and inspired.”
The Senior Games Program is a leading health promotion and well-being program for community members across Pitt County. During the Local Games, participants are joined by seniors from Beaufort, Martin, Hertford and Bertie Counties. Participants have year-round opportunities for practice sessions, clinic, and sports competition.
Greenville-Pitt County Senior Games is one of 54 local games sanctioned by North Carolina Senior Games. Select participants on the local level will be qualifying to compete in the N.C. Senior Games State Finals this fall.
The Greenville-Pitt County Senior Games are coordinated by Pitt County Cultural Arts & Recreation with the support and sponsorship of the Greenville Recreation and Parks Department, ECU Campus Recreation and Wellness Department, College of Health and Human Performance, Mid-East Commission Area Agency on Aging and Senior Services at ECU Health.
To register before March 5, visit torch.ncseniorgames.org or visit the Cultural Arts & Recreation office at 4561 County Home Rd, Greenville NC for a paper application!
The Mission of Pitt County Government is to enhance the health, safety, and well-being of our community by advocating for and providing quality services in a friendly and cost-effective manner. Learn more: www.PittCountyNC.gov
Media Contact: Dawn C. Jones, Director, Public Information & Media Relations | dawn.jones@pittcountync.gov | (252) 902-2993%20902-2993)

Pitt County, NC | Notice of Primary Election
Notice of Primary Election Pitt County, North Carolina
The statewide primary election will be held on Tuesday, March 3, 2026.
Voters will be asked to show photo ID when they vote. All voters will be allowed to vote with or without ID. Voters who lack ID can get one for free from their county board of elections. Find out more at ncsbe.gov/voter-id.
Polls will be open from 6:30 a.m. to 7:30 p.m. on Election Day.
Early voting will be held at the following locations from Thursday, February 12, 2026, through Saturday, February 28, 2026.Agricultural Center (403 Government Cir., Greenville)Center at Alice F. Keene Park (4561 County Home Rd., Greenville)County Office Building, PATS Conference Room (1717 W. 5th St., Greenville)ECU Student Center (501 E. 10th St., Greenville)Village of Simpson Town Hall (2768 Thompson St., Simpson)Winterville Fire Station, Community Room (2593 Railroad St., Winterville)
All listed sites will be open Weekdays from 8:00 a.m. – 7:30 p.m.; and Saturdays, February 14 and 28 from 8:00 a.m. – 3:00 p.m.
Absentee ballotswill be mailed to voters who have requested them beginning January 12, 2026 Absentee ballots must be received by the county board of elections no later than 7:30 p.m. on March 3, 2026. A voter can fill out an absentee ballot request at votebymail.ncsbe.gov, or by filling out a request form provided by the county board of elections office. The request must be received through the website or by the Pitt County Board of Elections by 5:00 p.m. February 17, 2026.
In the primary election, voters will select nominees for a political party to move on to the general election on November 3rd. Contests on the ballot include U. S. Senate, U.S. House, N.C. Supreme Court Associate Justice, N.C. Court of Appeals Judge, Superior and District Court Judge, N.C. House and Senate, and county offices. In the primary, voters affiliated with a political party will be given a ballot of candidates for their party, if their party has a primary. Unaffiliated voters may choose to vote in any party’s primary, but they may select only one party’s ballot.
The voter registration deadline for this election is 5:00 p.m. Friday, February 6, 2026. Eligible individuals who are not registered by that deadline may register and vote at any early voting site during the early voting period. Individuals registering to vote at an early voting site will be required to provide current documentation of their residence (for example, a government ID, other government document, or a paycheck, bank statement, or utility bill). Voters who wish to change party affiliation must do so by the February 6 deadline.
 Questions? Call the Pitt County Board of Elections Office at 252-902-3300or send an email to pittelections@pittcountync.gov.
Neal Driver, ChairPitt County Board of Elections

Economic Development | NCEDA Summer Fellow
Support Business Retention & Expansion Efforts
Pitt County Economic Development (PCED) seeks an NCEDA Summer Fellow to support the revitalization and modernization of its Business Retention and Expansion (BRE) program. This fellowship provides a unique opportunity to work directly with local industries, economic development professionals, and university partners to improve industry engagement and shape a new web-based BRE portal designed to enhance communication, connect resources, and strengthen the regional economy.
Examples of Job Duties
The primary duties of the NCEDA Fellow will be as followed: Research & Analysis
 Conduct research on best practices, models, and tools used for business retention and industry engagement portals nationwide.
 Review and analyze existing engagement strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and challenges (SWOC) across Pitt County.
Stakeholder Engagement
 Meet with the PCED team, ECU REDE, Greenville ENC Alliance, and other partners to understand BRE program goals, expected outcomes, and resource needs.
 Develop an engagement plan and interview framework to guide conversations with local industries.
 Coordinate, schedule, and facilitate meetings with industry partners and key stakeholders.
 Provide follow-up communication and documentation after each engagement session.
Data Gathering & Reporting
 Aggregate and summarize feedback gathered through industry meetings and stakeholder convenings.
 Prepare a comprehensive report detailing industry needs, engagement gaps, and opportunities for support and collaboration.
 Present findings to PCED leadership and stakeholders.
Portal Planning & Development
 Translate identified needs into an outline for a user-centered BRE engagement portal.
 Recommend features, content categories, and resource linkages that should be included on the portal.
 Collaborate with PCED, ECU, and development partners to refine portal scope and objectives. Implementation Plan Creation
 Develop recommendations for portal implementation, including: timeline, budget, data sources, roles, and sustainability.
 Present the final proposal and implementation plan to stakeholders for review and approval.
About Pitt County Economic Development
Pitt County, home to nearly 180,000 residents and anchored by Greenville, is a regional leader in healthcare, education, advanced manufacturing, biopharmaceuticals, and creative industries. PCED leads business recruitment, expansion, and retention efforts and collaborates with institutions like East Carolina University to drive regional economic growth.
Application deadline: January 30
Submit An Application Online Now

Pitt County Firefighters’ Association | Annual Awards
Pitt County Firefighters’ Association Awards
Awards presented to Firefighter, Fire Officer and Fire Chief of the Year
The Pitt County Firefighters’ Association annual “Ladies Night Dinner” was held Thursday, January 15, 2026 at Eastern Pine Fire-Rescue located at 5473 Eastern Pines Rd, Greenville, N.C. Three awards were presented following the meal.
Pitt County Firefighter of the Yearwas awarded to Cody Bateman of Simpson Rural Fire Department. The Pitt County Fire Officer of the Year award was presented to George W. Gay of Farmville Fire Department. Chief Thomas Wayne Lilleyof Bethel Volunteer Fire Department was named the Pitt County Fire Chief of the Year.
The awards are presented annually to individuals who have demonstrated outstanding support and leadership to their department and the community; contributing to the overall good and advancement of fire services in Pitt County for the previous 12 months. The selection committee is made up of individuals not currently in the Pitt County fire service.
“Pitt County Emergency Management joins the Firefighters’ Association in congratulating all award recipients and further thanking all of the dedicated individuals who serve the residents of Pitt County every day,” says Jay Morris, Fire Marshal / Emergency Management Deputy Director.
Emergency Management and the Firefighters’ Association are recruiting members for all rural volunteer fire departments. Volunteer firefighters are community members who defend residences and businesses from structure fire tragedies. Training/education may be completed within one year and is no cost to participants. Highschool students ages 14-17 years old, with parent/guardian permission, are encouraged to submit an interest form for the Junior Firefighter program. Interested individuals should visit www.PittCountyNC.gov/Firefighteror complete an Interest Form.
View Photo Gallery Now
For more information, contact PCEM at 252-902-3950.

Free Event | 2026 Community Unity Breakfast
The community is invited to attend the 2026 Community Unity Breakfasthosted by the Greenville-Pitt County Chamber of Commerce on Monday, January 19, 2026, from 7:30 a.m. to 9:00 a.m. at the ECU Murphy Center (Harvey Hall), located at 100 Ficklen Drive, Greenville, NC 27858.
Held in commemoration of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.’s birthday, the 29th Annual Community Unity Breakfast celebrates and promotes diversity initiatives throughout Greenville and Pitt County.
The keynote speaker will be Brian Floyd, Chief Operating Officer, ECU Health.
Thisevent is free and open to the public and may be standing-room only.
The Community Unity Breakfast is presented by the City of Greenville, ECU Health, Nutrien, and Reimage Church. Additional sponsors include Greenville Utilities Commission, 101.9 Kiss FM, Greenville-Pitt County Convention and Visitors Bureau, East Carolina University, Pitt Community College, and Pitt County Economic Development.
Questions? Please contact Aileen Peacock or call (252) 752-4101 ext. 2223.

Public Meeting Notice | Board of Equalization and Review
Public Meeting Notice
Pursuant to N.C.G.S. 105-322, thePitt County Board of Equalization and Review will meet as required by law. The purpose is to hear upon, request, any and all taxpayers who own or control taxable property assessed for taxation in Pitt County, with respect to the valuation of such property, or the property of others, and to fulfill other duties and responsibilities as required by law.
The Board will convene for its scheduled meeting on Thursday, January 29, 2026. The meeting will begin promptly at4:00 p.m. in the Eugene James Auditorium in the County Administration Building located at 1717 W. 5th Street, Greenville, N.C.
Request for hearing must be received no later than final adjournment for accepting new appeals, which is scheduled for Thursday, April 24, 2025 at 4:00 p.m.
In the event of an earlier or later adjournment, notice to that effect will be published in The Daily Reflector newspaper.
The schedule for the hearing of appeals which were timely filed will be posted at the office of the Assessor, serving as Clerk to the Board, and will also be provided to individuals and organizations that have requested notice pursuant to N.C.G.S. 143-318.12.
All requests for hearing should be made to: Russell D. Hill, Clerk to the Pitt County Board of Equalization and Review, Pitt County Tax Administration PO Box 43, Greenville, NC 27835-0043.
Any questions may be directed to the Clerk to the Board at 252-902-3400.
ADA accommodations:Please call 252-902-2950 at least 5 days before an event.

Our engineers create aerospace products and services that make the world better. Bring your ideas to life on Earth and beyond.

You and your team will build amazing products and work on advanced technologies that shape the future of manufacturing.

Senior Electronic Systems Design and Analysis Engineer
Job Description
At Boeing, we innovate and collaborate to make the world a better place. We’re committed to fostering an environment for every teammate that’s welcoming, respectful and inclusive, with great opportunity for professional growth. Find your future with us.
The Boeing Commercial Airplanes (BCA) is seeking multiple innovative and adaptable Senior Electronic Systems Design and Analysis Engineers to join our Integrated Cabin & Cargo Systems team in Everett, WA or Renton, WA.
Position Overview:
At Boeing, we are dedicated to defining and integrating advanced electrical systems across our commercial fleet. Our projects focus on translating high-level concepts and requirements into specific architectures and operational specifications, ensuring the seamless physical integration of electrical systems and components within our complex aircraft.
We collaborate with a global supplier network to implement cutting-edge system hardware and software, validating our designs through rigorous laboratory and in-flight testing. Our engineers work on aircraft systems from initial concept through certification, delivery, and throughout their production lifecycle, continually pushing the boundaries of innovation in commercial air transport.
This position will specifically involve working with Cabin Systems, which encompasses interior lighting, passenger address systems, seatback video, passenger connectivity, satellite connections, in-flight entertainment, and more. If this sounds like a great opportunity for you, submit your application today and join our team!
Senior (Level 4) applicants should be able to showcase significant technical depth and process knowledge in one or more Electronic Systems on the aircraft, and the ability to lead a team of engineers to a common goal.
Position Responsibilities:
Develops and documents electronic and electrical system requirements.
Designs hardware, software and interface specifications.
Tests and validates to ensure system designs meet operational and functional requirements.
Assists in monitoring supplier performance to ensure system integration and compliance with requirements.
Ensures compliance with appropriate regulations and certification requirements, including ARP4754A, DO-178, and DO-254.
Solves problems concerning fielded hardware and software over the entire product lifecycle.
Research specific technology advances for potential application to customer and company business needs.
Work Authorization:
This position is required to be 100% onsite. The selected candidate will be required to work onsite at Everett or Renton, WA.
Basic Qualifications (Required Skills/Experience):
Bachelor of Science degree in Engineering (with a focus in Electrical, Mechanical or Aeronautical), Computer Science, Data Science, Mathematics, Physics or Chemistry.
10+ years of experience working in an electrical design engineering environment (with emphasis on electronic and electrical systems design and analysis).
Preferred Qualifications (Desired Skills/Experience):
Experience within Electrical Systems hardware and software design.
Knowledge of FAA certification.
Experience with system design and integration during an airplane development program.
Experience with Model Based Development tools.
Conflict of Interest:
Successful candidates for this job must satisfy the Company’s Conflict of Interest (COI) assessment process.
Drug Free Workplace:
Boeing is a Drug Free Workplace where post offer applicants and employees are subject to testing for marijuana, cocaine, opioids, amphetamines, PCP, and alcohol when criteria is met as outlined in our policies.
Union:
This is a union-represented position.
Pay and Benefits:
At Boeing, we strive to deliver a Total Rewards package that will attract, engage and retain the top talent.  Elements of the Total Rewards package include competitive base pay and variable compensation opportunities.
The Boeing Company also provides eligible employees with an opportunity to enroll in a variety of benefit programs, generally including health insurance, flexible spending accounts, health savings accounts, retirement savings plans, life and disability insurance programs, and a number of programs that provide for both paid and unpaid time away from work.
The specific programs and options available to any given employee may vary depending on eligibility factors such as geographic location, date of hire, and the applicability of collective bargaining agreements.
Pay is based upon candidate experience and qualifications, as well as market and business considerations.
Summary Pay Range:
Senior (Level 4): $146,000 - $198,000
Applications for this position will be accepted until Apr. 13, 2026
Export Control Requirements:
This position must meet U.S. export control compliance requirements. To meet U.S. export control compliance requirements, a “U.S. Person” as defined by 22 C.F.R. §120.62 is required. “U.S. Person” includes U.S. Citizen, U.S. National, lawful permanent resident, refugee, or asylee.

US based job, US Person required

Program presentation
|  |

| Build your customized MBA    Customize your MBA to align with your personal and professional aspirations. Core courses will allow you to deep dive into strategic topics, while electives will enable you to personalize your program. In the meantime, you will choose up to two Career Learning Lab(s), and benefit from industry specific workshops, networking opportunities, and global learning expeditions. By the end of your curriculum, apply all your acquired skills to an internship, a venture project or a global field project. |
| Become a responsible business leader    Our MBA offers a unique curriculum. Through a set of core courses and electives designed to develop your leadership abilities, you will dive into our faculty’s cutting edge research and tackle the latest business challenges. Sustainability. Digital Leadership. Innovation and entrepreneurship. These 3 strategic topics constitute the first step to tackling societal and business challenges. |
| Unlock boundless opportunities in Europe & build a Global network  From the heart of Europe’s premier business quarter - Paris, La Défense - where your MBA will take place, to the key European business hubs visited during the global learning expeditions, benefit from exclusive and personalized events and opportunities to enhance your network and meet your career goals. |
| Reach beyond your career aspirations    ESSEC fosters collaborative leadership through "Learning by Doing". With a wide range of activities organized by the Career Learning Labs, from global learning expeditions to workshops, masterclasses, networking events, alumni get-togethers, a dedicated MBA team will offer personalized guidance and resources to tailor your journey to your specific career ambitions and trajectory. |
| Experience the power of a boutique-sized MBA    What is a boutique program? We select and welcome high-impact and high-potential, forward thinking and responsible individuals with a collaborative mindset and international backgrounds. The ultimate goal is to enhance your inner leader, provide you with the tools to accelerate your career and become a confident and responsible business leader ready to make an impact on business and society. |

Build your customized MBA
Customize your MBA to align with your personal and professional aspirations. Core courses will allow you to deep dive into strategic topics, while electives will enable you to personalize your program. In the meantime, you will choose up to two Career Learning Lab(s), and benefit from industry specific workshops, networking opportunities, and global learning expeditions. By the end of your curriculum, apply all your acquired skills to an internship, a venture project or a global field project.

Become a responsible business leader
Our MBA offers a unique curriculum. Through a set of core courses and electives designed to develop your leadership abilities, you will dive into our faculty’s cutting edge research and tackle the latest business challenges. Sustainability. Digital Leadership. Innovation and entrepreneurship. These 3 strategic topics constitute the first step to tackling societal and business challenges.

Unlock boundless opportunities in Europe & build a Global network
From the heart of Europe’s premier business quarter - Paris, La Défense - where your MBA will take place, to the key European business hubs visited during the global learning expeditions, benefit from exclusive and personalized events and opportunities to enhance your network and meet your career goals.

Reach beyond your career aspirations
ESSEC fosters collaborative leadership through "Learning by Doing". With a wide range of activities organized by the Career Learning Labs, from global learning expeditions to workshops, masterclasses, networking events, alumni get-togethers, a dedicated MBA team will offer personalized guidance and resources to tailor your journey to your specific career ambitions and trajectory.

Experience the power of a boutique-sized MBA
What is a boutique program? We select and welcome high-impact and high-potential, forward thinking and responsible individuals with a collaborative mindset and international backgrounds. The ultimate goal is to enhance your inner leader, provide you with the tools to accelerate your career and become a confident and responsible business leader ready to make an impact on business and society.
Obtain a certified “Grade Master” degree

Enrolling in the ESSEC Global MBA ensures that you will complete a program officially recognized by the French State as a “Grade Master” – an official BAC+5 degree – which certifies the quality of teaching delivered and the value of your degree. (RNCP niveau 7: RNCP41201)
This accreditation also enables international students to apply for a work permit in France upon graduation. This work permit is typically valid for one year and can be extended under specific conditions.
1+1 Degree Opportunities

ESSEC Global MBA program has partnered with the Yale School of Management (SOM) to offer a new 1+1 degree pathway: the Global MBA + Master of Advanced Management (MAM). To participate, students must first be admitted and enrolled at ESSEC. In the following year, they can apply to Yale to complete the MAM. In just two years, participants earn twodegrees, broaden their global perspective, deepen their skills, and join a worldwide network of peers and professionals.

For more information on 1+1 degree opportunities, reach out to your advisor to learn about the application process.

### David Sluss

The world needs innovative, responsible, and digital-savvy leaders – leaders who are ready to enlighten, lead, and change the world. The ESSEC Global MBA program is designed to take you on a transformative journey in becoming a global leader in which you will experience a high-impact and high-return career.

ESSEC’s next-generation, personalized curriculum focusing on sustainability, digital leadership, and innovation & entrepreneurship goes hand-in-hand with powerful career learning labs.
These one-of-a-kind career learning labs put your personalized career aspirations to work in one (or two) high-growth options: luxury, finance, consulting, or product management.

We are a boutique program. We bring in a relatively small, high impact, collaborative, and global cohort every year.

Our aim is to partner with you as you accelerate your career and your leadership capacities!

The world needs global business leaders who can resiliently face the great challenges and seize the opportunities of today’s economy: sustainability, digitalization, and innovation. With an ESSEC Global MBA, you will become the global leader the world needs. We partner with you to provide a globally integrated journey that transforms you into an innovative, responsible, and digital-savvy leader prepared to succeed in high-return and high-impact careers.  
  
The ESSEC Global MBA integrates sustainability, digitalization, and innovation across core courses. You also can personalize your journey by “mixing and matching” electives across these areas.

|  |

| Sustainability  Businesses are experiencing a renewed sense of responsibility for both social and ecological challenges. With this shift comes a committed search for business-trained talent with a deep knowledge of these challenges as well as the best tools and practices for increasing sustainability.    Example courses: Sustainable transformation, Circular Economy in Supply Change, Sustainable Marketing, Sustainable Finance, etc.  Targeted roles: ESG (Environment, Society, and Governance) Consulting, Leading a Sustainable Supply Chain Function, or Analyzing Impact Investing, etc |
| Digital Leadership  Digital is “the” way of doing business now and for the future. Businesses need not only digital skills, but also strategic talent that can lead organizations in aligning digital strategy with digital transformation.    Example courses: Implementing AI, Fintech, Digital Marketing, and/or How to lead digital transformations, etc.  Targeted roles: Digital Product Manager, Digital Strategy Consultant, or Business Intelligence Manager, etc |
| Innovation & Entrepreneurship  Growth and innovation are inherent aspects of business. Therefore, businesses require talent with the skills to lead innovation and growth at large organizations, as well as leaders who can work with fast-growing startups and scale-ups – while possibly becoming entrepreneurs themselves.    Example courses: Entrepreneurial Finance, Growing a Brand, Innovation Management, etc.  Targeted roles: Venture Capital Analyst, Brand Leader, or Innovation Consultant, etc |

 Sustainability

Businesses are experiencing a renewed sense of responsibility for both social and ecological challenges. With this shift comes a committed search for business-trained talent with a deep knowledge of these challenges as well as the best tools and practices for increasing sustainability.

 Digital Leadership

Digital is “the” way of doing business now and for the future. Businesses need not only digital skills, but also strategic talent that can lead organizations in aligning digital strategy with digital transformation.

Innovation & Entrepreneurship

Growth and innovation are inherent aspects of business. Therefore, businesses require talent with the skills to lead innovation and growth at large organizations, as well as leaders who can work with fast-growing startups and scale-ups – while possibly becoming entrepreneurs themselves.

Across these three key topics, you may have a career goal of contributing more specifically in roles aligned in Finance. The ESSEC Global MBA allows you to choose a Finance elective cluster. This is the power of the ESSEC Global MBA! The power to personalize your career journey across, industry sectors, and roles.

Accelerate your career by applying your knowledge

By embracing agility, opt for diverse experiential pathways that enable hands-on learning, international exposure and entrepreneurial exploration. Consider internships, global field projects or venturing projects.

|  |

| Internships  The internship will enable you to gain practical experience in the specific field of your choice, applying theoretical knowledge, acquiring new skills, and developing your professional networks. |
| Global Field Project  The Global Field project is specifically designed to give you hands-on experience working with a team on a real-life business problem, enabling you to apply and demonstrate your newly acquired skills and knowledge. Depending on your interest, the topic is defined in collaboration with sponsoring companies. |
| Venturing Project  Do you have a business idea you want to bring to life? Reveal your inner entrepreneur! At ESSEC, Entrepreneurship is in our DNA. In the Venturing Project, you will take your business idea and go through ESSEC Ventures' 'lean startup' process to create a viable and scalable business model - setting you up for success. Given the project's high-risk / high-reward nature, we take only a select number from each cohort. Your project will receive support via:    Coaching from ESSEC Ventures' experienced advisors  Mentoring from ESSEC Ventures' extensive network of successful alumni entrepreneurs  Possible admission to be incubated via ESSEC's incubator at Station F - the world's largest start-up incubator |

Develop your inner leader

From day one, you will focus on developing your ability to enlighten, lead, and change the world around you. At the ESSEC Global MBA, we believe that a more sustainable, digitally-connected, and innovative world requires leaders to integrate collaboration and competition as well as creativity and execution.

French Culture Integration  
During three days of integration into the ESSEC Global MBA, you will be immersed in leadership challenges. The location may change from one year to the next. Over the course of this three-day adventure, you will form a strong bond with the other members of the cohort. This bond will carry you through the year and well into your future.

The "Toward Responsible Innovation" missionThrough signature developmental experiences, we implement a “do, reflect, and learn” approach to building your leadership competencies. In the “Towards Responsible Innovation” mission, you will be immersed in sustainability issues and leadership challenges. ESSEC takes great pride in exploring new training and teaching techniques. In order to stay abreast of current sustainability trends and issues, the mission and partner company will change from one year to the next.

Global Business SimulationYou will put into practice ESSEC’s belief that the grand business challenges can only be met via collaborative and ‘future-forward’ leadership.

You will also be coached through this process. At the beginning of your ESSEC Global MBA journey, you will participate in a 360-degree leadership assessment, make personalized leadership improvement goals, and receive feedback and coaching throughout your journey to track your progress.

Open
Close

Experience hands-on learning

Field trips will enable you to understand contemporary business challenges in different socio-economic and cultural contexts. During the second term, you will embark on a week-long field trip, the location of which changes year to year. This field trip is a mix of cultural and professional activities to help you explore how business is conducted in different countries. During this trip, you will complete an industry-focused research project, visit companies, and meet local ESSEC alumni. This year, business trips are organized to key European destinations.

Field trip destinations may be subject to change.

Our full-time Global MBA is designed for responsible and high-potential professionals looking to make a career leap through a 12-month intensive and life-changing journey.

Requirements:

Age: At least 25 years old when you submit your application (average: 30)

Work experience: At least 3 years of post-university professional experience (excluding internships)

International: International work experience (abroad or in an international environment)

Degree: A 4-year Bachelor's Degree\

Aptitude management test: A competitive GMAT/Executive Assessment/GRE or Tage-Mage score\

English level: An advanced level of English

\For candidates holding a 3-year Bachelor’s degree, a specific VAPP (Validation of Acquired Professional and Personal Knowledge) process applies. A minimum of 5 years of professional experience is required to be eligible.

\For specific 1+1 degree opportunities with Yale, please contact your program advisor to discuss the requirements further.

\Please bear in mind that you don't need your GMAT/Executive Assessment/GRE or Tage-Mage score to start your GMBA journey so don't wait to contact our team of advisors and discuss your profile, project and application!

Our team of advisors will be happy to review your profile and answer any questions you may have.

The First 90 Days: A Practical 30-60-90 Day Plan for Product Managers
The First 90 Days: A Practical 30-60-90 Day Plan for Product Managers
Gregg Gellhaus, CBAP, CPOSP
Gregg Gellhaus, CBAP, CPOSP
Machinify•882 followers
Machinify•882 followers
Starting a new role as a Product Manager can feel like being handed the keys to a moving car. There’s pressure to deliver quickly, but real impact comes from learning the business, earning trust, and making informed decisions. A thoughtful 30-60-90-day plan helps balance urgency with effectiveness.
Here’s what a strong first-90-day plan should look like for a Product Manager joining a new organization.
Days 1–30: Learn, Listen, and Build Context
The first month is about understanding before acting. Your primary job is to absorb information and establish credibility through curiosity and clarity.
Focus areas:
• Understand the company’s mission, strategy, and success metrics
• Learn the product portfolio, customers, and market positioning
• Build relationships with engineering, design, sales, marketing, and leadership
• Get familiar with existing roadmaps, backlogs, and delivery processes
Key activities:
• Conduct stakeholder interviews to understand expectations and pain points
• Review customer feedback, support tickets, and sales insights
• Study analytics, KPIs, and financial drivers tied to the product
• Observe team rituals and decision-making norms
Outcomes by Day 30:
• Clear understanding of the product’s purpose and value proposition
• A map of key stakeholders and decision influencers
• Identified gaps, risks, and opportunities (without prescribing solutions yet)
Days 31–60: Analyze, Align, and Prioritize
The second phase is about turning knowledge into direction. You should begin forming opinions, validating assumptions, and aligning stakeholders around priorities.
Focus areas:
• Evaluate the current product strategy and roadmap
• Identify quick wins and high-impact opportunities
• Clarify roles, ownership, and decision frameworks
Key activities:
• Validate problems using data and customer insights
Recommended by LinkedIn
Level Up Your Product Game: A 90-Day Plan
The Science and The Art of Prioritization and Trade-offs in Product Management
Why Great Product Managers Don’t Chase Features — They Chase Impact
• Pressure-test the roadmap against business goals and user needs
• Collaborate with engineering to understand technical constraints and velocity
• Begin refining the backlog with clearer outcomes and acceptance criteria
Outcomes by Day 60:
• A prioritized view of what matters most and why
• Alignment with leadership on near-term goals
• Improved clarity for the delivery team on priorities and success metrics
Days 61–90: Execute, Influence, and Drive Outcomes
The final phase is about delivering value and demonstrating leadership. By now, you should be trusted enough to make decisions and move initiatives forward.
Focus areas:
• Execute against agreed priorities
• Improve product processes and communication
• Establish a forward-looking product narrative
Key activities:
• Launch or advance key initiatives tied to measurable outcomes
• Introduce improvements to discovery, planning, or feedback loops
• Communicate progress and trade-offs transparently
• Set a clear 6–12-month product vision or roadmap
Outcomes by Day 90:
• Tangible product progress with measurable impact
• Strong working relationships and decision credibility
• A clear, shared understanding of where the product is headed
A strong 30-60-90-day plan is not a checklist or a race to ship features. It is a deliberate progression from understanding to influence to impact. The most effective Product Managers resist the urge to “fix” things too quickly and instead focus on building shared context, aligning incentives, and grounding decisions in real customer and business signals.
By the end of the first 90 days, success is not measured by how much you changed, but by how clearly the organization understands its product direction, how confidently teams can execute, and how well trade-offs are made. Trust, clarity, and credibility compound over time—and the groundwork for all three is set in those first few months.
When approached with intention, the first 90 days don’t just onboard a Product Manager; they establish a product leader.
Like
Celebrate
Support
Love
Insightful
Funny

K'Ronn Cranford, MBA, graphic
Cranford Digital Advisory•4K followers
I really enjoy the first 30 days being a process of Learn, Listen, and Build Context. You can make much more informed and impactful decisions when you’ve taken time to absorb your new environment.
I also think this phase is a great phase to dispel any assumptions you may have about the product, company, and what value truly means.
