Evaluating Onboarding Software: 15 Must-Have Requirements for 2026
Published on
February 2, 2026
Updated on
Category
Employee Onboarding
Copy link
Onboarding as a Strategic Investment in 2026
Losing a new hire is costly ,  replacing an employee can cost up to three or four times their salary. This hard fact has elevated onboarding from an administrative checklist to a strategic priority. Modern organizations recognize that how they integrate new team members has long-term business impact on retention, productivity, and culture. Research shows that companies with strong onboarding processes improve new-hire retention by as much as 82%, with corresponding gains of over 70% in productivity. In an era of persistent talent shortages and hybrid work models, effective onboarding is a must-have business strategy to secure talent and accelerate time-to-competency.
The onboarding landscape in 2026 is defined by digital ecosystems and data-driven practices. It’s not just about welcoming an employee on day one ,  it’s about providing an immersive experience that spans pre-hire to well beyond the first month. Organizations are investing in sophisticated onboarding software to streamline workflows and deliver personalized, engaging journeys for every new hire. Below, we outline 15 essential requirements decision-makers should look for when evaluating onboarding software solutions for 2026, each tying into broader strategic outcomes for the enterprise.
Table of Contents
 1. Seamless Integration with HR and IT Systems
 2. Automated Workflows and Paperless Forms
 3. Personalized, Role-Based Journeys
 4. Pre-Boarding Engagement Tools
 5. Remote-Friendly and Mobile-First Experience
 6. Continuous Learning and Development Modules
 7. Gamification for New Hire Engagement
 8. Social Connection and Collaboration Features
 9. Manager and Leadership Involvement
 10. Analytics and Performance Tracking
 11. Feedback and Continuous Improvement Loops
 12. Compliance and Risk Management
 13. Inclusive and Accessible Design
 14. AI-Powered Assistance and Insights
 15. Scalability, Security, and Cloud Reliability
 Final Thoughts: Making Onboarding a Competitive Advantage
 Elevating Your Onboarding Strategy with TechClass
Seamless Integration with HR and IT Systems
Any top-tier onboarding platform in 2026 must function as part of a larger HR technology ecosystem. The software should integrate with core HRIS, payroll, and identity management systems to ensure data flows smoothly from recruiting into onboarding and beyond. Seamless integration eliminates redundant data entry, reduces errors, and triggers cross-functional processes automatically. For example, syncing with IT provisioning tools means new hires get email accounts, equipment, and access permissions without delay. This prevents costly productivity lags ,  nearly half of employees have reported waiting over a week for basic work tools or system access when onboarding. An integrated onboarding solution prepares everything behind the scenes so that a new employee’s first day is focused on people and learning, not paperwork glitches. In addition, integration with collaboration platforms (email, calendars, chat apps) helps incorporate the new hire into the company’s communication flows from day one. The organization benefits through greater efficiency and consistency: information is updated in all systems, HR compliance is tighter, and the new hire experiences a unified, professional welcome.
Automated Workflows and Paperless Forms
Modern onboarding software should automate routine workflows and eliminate manual paperwork. Short-staffed HR teams cannot afford to spend hours chasing forms and tracking tasks. Automation ensures that every step ,  from sending out welcome packets to scheduling orientation sessions ,  is handled through predefined workflows. Tasks can be auto-assigned to IT, facilities, or managers, and the software sends reminders so nothing falls through the cracks. Critically, the platform must support digital form completion with e-signatures, turning formerly tedious paperwork (tax forms, NDAs, I-9s) into a quick online experience. Going paperless not only saves time but also demonstrates respect for the new hire’s time by avoiding day-one form marathons. In fact, 58% of companies admit they focus heavily on processes and paperwork during onboarding ,  an imbalance that can crowd out more meaningful activities. By automating administrative tasks, organizations free their HR staff to focus on personal interactions and coaching. This translates into real cost savings and scalability: one company saved over $150,000 per year by automating onboarding provisioning and paperwork processing. An effective onboarding system acts as an “HR co-pilot,” handling the busywork so that the enterprise can onboard more hires faster without sacrificing quality or compliance.
Manual vs. Automated Onboarding
Shifting from administrative lag to strategic engagement
Traditional Process
Automated Workflow
🐌 Slow & Manual  
Hours spent chasing paper forms and correcting data entry errors.
⚡ Instant & Digital  
Workflows auto-assign tasks; e-signatures complete paperwork instantly.
📉 Productivity Lag  
New hires wait ~1 week for tools; Day 1 is consumed by admin tasks.
🚀 Day 1 Ready  
IT/Accounts ready on arrival; focus shifts to coaching and culture.
💰 Real-World Impact: Can save over $150,000/year in admin costs
Personalized, Role-Based Journeys
No two new employees have identical needs. Onboarding software in 2026 must deliver personalized onboarding journeys tailored to each role, department, and even individual background. Instead of a one-size-fits-all checklist, the system should curate relevant training modules, introductions, and milestones for the specific position. For example, a sales hire might receive product training and peer shadowing tasks, while an engineering hire gets access to code repositories and technical onboarding sessions. Personalization drives faster time-to-productivity ,  new hires reach competence sooner when their onboarding content fits their job context. AI-powered platforms make this feasible at scale: advanced systems can dynamically adjust content based on a new hire’s experience level or learning pace. The impact is significant for the business. A tailored approach helps newcomers feel the company’s investment in their success, fostering engagement. It also avoids underloading or overwhelming employees by giving the “just right” amount of information at the right time. By 2025, an estimated 81% of organizations planned to invest in AI-driven onboarding technology to enable deeply personalized experiences. The must-have capability here is a rules-based or AI-driven engine in the software that can assign onboarding tasks and content conditionally ,  ensuring a custom journey that aligns with the employee’s role, location, and even learning style.
Pre-Boarding Engagement Tools
Onboarding now begins before the first day. High-performing organizations are 53% more likely to start engaging new hires during the pre-boarding period (after offer acceptance but before start date). Therefore, great onboarding software includes tools to keep future hires warm and excited in this interim. This can range from sending a personalized welcome portal or video message, to providing access to an early onboarding checklist or company FAQs. Such features serve a strategic purpose: they maintain the candidate’s commitment and drastically reduce “no-shows” or reneged offers. (Notably, Gartner research found about 50% of candidates who accept offers may later decide not to show up, often due to a lack of connection before day one.) A robust pre-boarding module might allow managers to introduce themselves via a quick note, ship necessary equipment in advance, or even assign a “pre-day-one” learning module to familiarize the hire with company values. These early touchpoints help new employees feel valued and prepared, lowering their anxiety. By the time they officially start, they already have a sense of belonging. For the enterprise, using pre-boarding as a retention strategy pays off in higher new-hire yield and faster integration. In evaluating onboarding solutions, look for features like automated pre-start communication workflows, content portals for incoming hires, and the ability to handle provisioning of accounts or hardware ahead of day one.
The "Pre-Boarding" Opportunity Window
1
Offer Accepted
Candidate commits to the role. Risk of "No-show" exists here.
2
Pre-Boarding Engagement
📝 Admin: Send digital forms & I-9s
📹 Culture: Send welcome video & FAQs
💻 IT: Provision email & hardware
3
Day One: Connected
Employee arrives prepared, equipped, and feeling valued.
"High-performing orgs are 53% more likely to engage during pre-boarding."
Remote-Friendly and Mobile-First Experience
With remote and hybrid work now standard, onboarding software must be designed for geographically dispersed teams. This means providing a rich, interactive experience for a new hire who might be joining from home or another country. A remote-friendly onboarding platform supports virtual orientation sessions (integrating with video conferencing), digital office tours, and online introductions to team members. Importantly, it should facilitate cultural integration at a distance ,  for example, by showcasing company stories, values, and informal channels to help a remote hire feel the company’s personality. The challenge is real: remote new hires are nearly 50% more likely to say that company culture wasn’t effectively conveyed during onboarding compared to on-site hires. The right software addresses this via features like virtual team coffee chats, mentor/buddy pairing (with prompts to meet virtually), and interactive org charts to learn who is who. Moreover, mobile access is essential. Busy new employees should be able to complete onboarding tasks or training on their smartphone or tablet, not just a company computer. A mobile-first design lets them sign forms, watch welcome videos, or review checklists on the go, accommodating the modern, flexible workstyle. This is particularly vital for deskless workers or those in the field. By choosing an onboarding solution that is device-agnostic and optimized for remote connectivity, the organization ensures every hire ,  whether in headquarters or remote ,  gets a consistent, engaging start. The outcome is higher engagement and less drop-off; even fully remote employees report feeling more connected when the onboarding process uses technology to bridge the physical gap.
Continuous Learning and Development Modules
Onboarding doesn’t end with HR policies ,  it is the launching point for an employee’s learning journey at the company. In 2026, a must-have feature is integration of training and development content right into the onboarding process. Leading organizations use onboarding software to deliver role-specific training, whether it’s product knowledge, compliance courses, or foundational skills, in a timely manner. By weaving learning into onboarding, companies can “certify” new hires on core competencies and standard procedures early on. This proactive approach addresses skills gaps and prepares the workforce faster for upcoming challenges. The software should therefore connect with or include a Learning Management System (LMS) or microlearning library. Microlearning capabilities ,  breaking down training into bite-sized, 5, 10 minute modules ,  are particularly valuable for new hires who are absorbing lots of information. It keeps learning engaging and digestible, rather than overwhelming. Additionally, interactive elements like quizzes or simulations can reinforce understanding and give managers insight into a new hire’s progress. Investing in onboarding platforms with built-in learning content or seamless LMS integration has clear payoffs: companies with comprehensive training during onboarding see significantly higher long-term satisfaction and retention, as employees feel set up for success and personal growth. In fact, providing excellent training and development opportunities is cited by 35% of employees as a key factor that makes an employer attractive. For the organization, this requirement ensures that onboarding accelerates time-to-competency, directly impacting productivity and performance in the first critical months.
Gamification for New Hire Engagement
Early engagement is vital for new hires, and gamification features have proven effective in making the onboarding journey more fun and motivating. Onboarding software should ideally offer gamified elements such as achievement badges, points for completing tasks, progress bars, or friendly competitions (e.g. a leaderboard of who finished certain learning modules). These mechanics tap into basic human psychology ,  people are more driven to complete activities when there are goals and rewards involved. For example, awarding a “Certified Team Player” badge after finishing orientation training or giving points for each onboarding task done can instill a sense of accomplishment. Some organizations have implemented gamified onboarding with great success: global companies like Cisco and Microsoft saw increased employee engagement by incorporating game elements into training. The value to the enterprise is higher completion rates of onboarding activities and more energized, interactive participation from new hires. Gamification can also encourage exploration; a new hire might be more likely to click through optional learning resources if they’re presented as challenges or quests. When evaluating an onboarding platform, leaders should check for configurable gamification options ,  the ability to define milestones, offer recognition, and perhaps social features (like new hires seeing each other’s progress in a cohort). Used judiciously, gamification supports the ultimate goal of making onboarding memorable and motivating, rather than a passive checklist.
Read also:
Cost-Benefit Analysis of Outsourcing Employee Onboarding?
Evaluating Onboarding Software: 15 Must-Have Requirements for 2026
Justifying Onboarding Software: 5 Financial Arguments to Present to Your CFO

Social Connection and Collaboration Features
Joining a new organization is not just about tasks ,  it’s about people. Thus, a must-have in onboarding software is functionality that fosters social connections for the new hire. This can include a built-in “meet the team” section, where new employees can see profiles of their teammates, or even integration with corporate social networks or chat channels to say hello. Many modern onboarding tools allow assignment of a buddy or mentor and facilitate their introduction. For instance, the system might automatically pair each new hire with a volunteer buddy in their department and prompt that buddy to reach out in the first week. Such features address a critical aspect of assimilation: feeling connected to colleagues. They also reflect an organizational strategy of building networks for newcomers, which has been shown to improve engagement and retention. Enterprises should look for software that supports team welcomes, whether through group video welcome calls or collaborative onboarding activities (like new hire group projects or discussion forums for a cohort of hires). According to one survey, employees rated “connections to colleagues and peers” and “connection to managers” among the most beneficial aspects of onboarding technology. This indicates that facilitating human interaction ,  even via digital means ,  is key for a positive onboarding experience. In practice, an onboarding platform might trigger a team introduction workflow: notifying the team of the new hire’s arrival, sharing fun facts about the new person, or organizing a virtual meet-and-greet. By ensuring the software enables these social touchpoints, the organization cultivates a sense of belonging from the outset, which pays dividends in collaboration and morale.
Manager and Leadership Involvement
An often underutilized but crucial element of onboarding is active participation from managers and leaders. The software should therefore make it easy (and standard practice) for the hiring manager, team leaders, and even senior executives to be part of the onboarding process. This can take the form of automated reminders or checklists for managers ,  for example, prompting the hiring manager to schedule a 30-day check-in or to take the new hire to lunch (in-person or virtually) during week one. It could also include templated welcome messages from the CEO or department head that the system delivers to each newcomer. The reason this is so important is clear: only about one-third of organizations currently involve leadership in onboarding programs, yet those that do see better alignment and new-hire confidence. A personal welcome or time invested by leaders signals to the new employee that the organization values them. It helps convey the company’s vision and how the individual’s role contributes to larger goals ,  something a direct manager is best positioned to explain. Onboarding software can institutionalize these practices by integrating them into the workflow (e.g., “Day 1: Manager introduces team and sets expectations” as a required step). Furthermore, leaders can use the platform to share messages about company culture or strategy via video, which scales a warm welcome to all new hires globally. When choosing an onboarding solution, ensure it includes manager dashboards or tools that guide those managers through their onboarding responsibilities. The platform should treat managers and executives as users in the process, not just HR and the new hire. By doing so, organizations foster leadership accountability for onboarding ,  a factor that strongly correlates with higher new-hire engagement and performance.
Analytics and Performance Tracking
In 2026, data-driven decision making is everywhere, and onboarding is no exception. An effective onboarding platform must provide robust analytics and reporting capabilities so HR and business leaders can track the program’s performance. Key metrics to look for include completion rates of onboarding tasks, time-to-productivity (how quickly new hires reach certain performance milestones), and engagement levels with onboarding content. By monitoring these indicators, organizations can spot bottlenecks or drop-off points in their process.
Essential Onboarding KPIs
Core metrics to measure program health and ROI
Task Completion
Identifies bottlenecks (e.g., finding that only 60% complete training).
Time-to-Productivity
45 Days (Avg)
Measures speed to full performance benchmarks.
Engagement Risk
High Priority
Flags drop-offs in content interaction early.
For example, analytics might reveal that only 60% of new hires complete all assigned training modules, or that the average new hire takes 45 days to become fully productive in a certain role. These insights are invaluable for continuous improvement ,  they allow the company to tweak the onboarding program, allocate support where needed, or set targets for improvement. Surprisingly, over half of organizations still do not formally measure onboarding effectiveness, representing a huge missed opportunity. Best-in-class onboarding solutions will include customizable dashboards and the ability to slice data by department, location, cohort, etc. Some even leverage predictive analytics: using past engagement data to predict which new hires might be at risk of low performance or early turnover. For instance, if a new employee isn’t engaging with required training or is missing checkpoints, the system could flag this for HR intervention. Ultimately, having solid analytics ensures onboarding is managed as a strategic process with KPIs, not just a one-time event. When evaluating software, decision-makers should require clear reporting on ROI-related metrics ,  like retention rates of those who went through a new onboarding program versus previous rates ,  to tangibly demonstrate the business value of investing in a modern platform.
Feedback and Continuous Improvement Loops
Great onboarding software facilitates two-way feedback: not only does the company track the new hire’s progress, but it also gathers input from the new hires about their experience. Creating feedback loops is a must-have because it turns onboarding into an iterative, improving cycle rather than a static program. Look for features such as automated surveys or check-in forms that are sent to employees at various intervals ,  for example, after the first week, 30 days, 90 days, and again at 6 months.
The Continuous Feedback Loop
Strategic check-in points to reduce early turnover
Week 1: Initial Welcome
Assess preparedness, resource access, and first impressions.
Day 30: The "Risk Zone" Check-in
Identify early frustrations. (Up to 20% of turnover happens in the first 45 days.)
Day 90: Performance Review
Evaluate role alignment and transition from "new hire" to contributor.
Month 6: Development & Growth
Shift focus to long-term career pathing and retention.
These surveys can capture how welcomed and prepared new hires felt, which resources were helpful, and what challenges they faced. It’s important to note that up to 20% of employee turnover can happen within the first 45 days. By soliciting feedback early, organizations can identify issues that might be causing frustration or misalignment and address them before they result in an exit. Moreover, many companies overlook this step ,  one study found 55% of organizations don’t measure onboarding outcomes at all. A software solution that bakes in feedback collection ensures this critical data isn’t left to ad-hoc conversations. Continuous onboarding is another emerging concept: rather than viewing “day 90” as the end, some programs extend structured support through an employee’s first year (with periodic development check-ins and goal setting). In practice, the platform could be used to prompt managers to conduct a formal 90-day performance review or a career development discussion at the 6-month mark. By doing so, the company reinforces growth and shows employees that their onboarding and integration is truly a year-long (or longer) development journey, echoing expert recommendations to extend onboarding through the first year for maximum retention impact. In sum, the ideal onboarding system is one that not only guides new hires but also listens to them, and then helps translate that feedback into program enhancements over time.
Compliance and Risk Management
Onboarding software must cover the compliance essentials to protect the organization legally and ensure every new hire meets requirements. This includes managing government forms (tax withholding documents, employment eligibility verifications like I-9 in the US, nondisclosure agreements, etc.) as well as company policy acknowledgments (codes of conduct, safety guidelines, data security policies). A strong platform will provide secure digital workflows for these documents, with auditing capabilities to confirm each required item is completed. For instance, the system should track that a new hire has submitted their signed confidentiality agreement and completed any mandatory compliance training (such as anti-harassment training), flagging any missing pieces automatically. The benefit is twofold: it reduces administrative overhead in chasing down paperwork, and it reduces compliance risk by ensuring nothing is overlooked. Good onboarding software can also stay updated with changing regulations ,  for example, if labor laws require a new form or an update, the software vendor may handle those updates centrally (a perk of SaaS solutions). Additionally, security is a component of compliance: the platform will handle a lot of personal data (addresses, bank info for payroll, possibly background check results), so it must have enterprise-grade security and privacy controls. Evaluators should check for features like encryption, role-based access (so only authorized staff see sensitive info), and clear data retention policies. Another aspect is workflow compliance ,  making sure that IT and Facilities processes (like account setup or ergonomic assessments for remote setups) happen reliably for each hire. An automated onboarding system can enforce these process checkpoints and provide an audit trail. Ultimately, this requirement is about shielding the enterprise from legal pitfalls and ensuring every employee is properly documented and trained as required. In 2026’s environment of stringent data protection and workplace regulations, a lack of compliance features is a deal-breaker when choosing onboarding software.
Inclusive and Accessible Design
An often overlooked requirement, but one gaining prominence, is that onboarding software should be inclusive and accessible to all employees. This means the platform’s design and content should accommodate different languages, cultures, and abilities. For global organizations, multilingual support is essential ,  the system should allow new hires to experience onboarding materials in their preferred or local language. Ensuring that interfaces and videos have translations or subtitles demonstrates cultural respect and improves comprehension for non-native speakers. Accessibility features are equally important: the software should comply with standards (like WCAG for web accessibility), enabling usage by those with visual, hearing, or other impairments. This could involve having screen reader compatibility, high-contrast display options, closed captioning on all video content, and alternatives to purely visual content for those who might have difficulty. An inclusive approach also considers different learning styles and neurodiversity ,  providing content in multiple formats (text, video, interactive) so each individual can engage in the way they learn best. Why does this matter to the organization? Because a truly effective onboarding process makes every single new hire feel welcome and supported, not just the majority. If any group finds the onboarding platform hard to use or alienating, the company risks disengaging talent and possibly missing out on contributions. Beyond the ethical imperative, there’s a practical one: diverse hiring is at an all-time high, and companies know that embracing diversity means ensuring all employees can equally access information and opportunities from day one. When assessing onboarding software, leaders should verify that the vendor prioritizes accessibility compliance and offers configuration for multi-language content. Inclusivity isn’t a “nice-to-have” ,  it’s a must-have for enterprises that operate across regions and strive to be employers of choice in a diverse workforce.
AI-Powered Assistance and Insights
Artificial intelligence has become a game-changer in HR technology, and onboarding is reaping the benefits. An advanced onboarding solution for 2026 will leverage AI-driven features to enhance the new-hire experience and provide intelligent insights to the organization. One practical example is AI chatbots or virtual assistants embedded in the platform. These can answer common questions for new hires 24/7 (“How do I set up direct deposit?” or “What’s the dress code?”) in a conversational manner, reducing the load on HR and providing instant help to employees. This is especially useful for large or global companies where new hires might have questions outside of HR’s business hours. AI can also personalize content delivery: by analyzing a new hire’s progress and feedback, the system might recommend additional resources or adjust the pacing of modules (akin to a personalized tutor). Another valuable aspect is predictive analytics ,  AI algorithms can examine onboarding engagement data and potentially predict outcomes like performance or retention risks. For instance, if a new hire shows low interaction with onboarding materials and low survey scores, the AI might flag them as a flight risk, allowing HR to intervene early. According to industry analysis, a vast majority of organizations are embracing these technologies ,  over 80% plan to use AI-powered onboarding tech to deliver personalized, predictive experiences. The payoff can be substantial: companies using AI report streamlining onboarding processes by saving days of manual work and reducing delays. In one case, Hitachi introduced an AI assistant that cut their onboarding cycle by four days through automation and around-the-clock support. When considering onboarding platforms, decision-makers should inquire about the AI capabilities on offer ,  whether it’s natural language chat support, smart content curation, or predictive analytics dashboards. Adopting AI in onboarding helps the organization scale high-touch onboarding in a cost-effective way and stay ahead with continuous improvements driven by data patterns.
Scalability, Security, and Cloud Reliability
Finally, any onboarding software chosen in 2026 must meet the enterprise’s technical and scalability demands. As organizations grow and adapt, the platform should seamlessly handle increasing volume ,  whether it’s onboarding 50 employees a year or 5,000. Scalability also implies the software can support multiple business units, locations, or hiring surges (such as seasonal hiring waves) without performance issues. Cloud-based Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) solutions are now the norm in this space, and for good reason. A cloud onboarding platform provides regular updates, ensures users always have the latest features (like new compliance form versions), and reduces the burden on internal IT for maintenance. It also enables new hires to access the system from anywhere, which is critical in the remote work era. When evaluating, look at the reliability track record ,  uptime commitments, load capacity, and mobile responsiveness all matter for a smooth experience. Security is non-negotiable: the system will house personal identifiable information and possibly sensitive documents, so it must have strong encryption, secure hosting, and compliance with data protection regulations (GDPR, etc.). Vendors should offer clarity on how they protect data and allow organizations to configure permissions. Additionally, integration capability (as discussed in point #1) contributes to scalability; a scalable platform plays well in a larger tech stack via APIs and integration hubs. Another angle is configurability ,  the software should be flexible enough to accommodate the organization’s evolving processes (for example, if you introduce a new mentorship program in onboarding, can the software workflow be adjusted easily?). By selecting a solution that is robust and secure under the hood, companies ensure that their onboarding improvements are sustainable for the long term. Investing in a proven, cloud-based platform also signals to new hires that the company is modern and serious about their employee experience, reinforcing the positive first impression. In summary, the technical foundation of the onboarding tool must be as strong as the functional features, so that HR leaders aren’t constrained by system limitations as their strategy grows.
Final Thoughts: Making Onboarding a Competitive Advantage
The 15 requirements outlined above underscore that evaluating onboarding software is no longer a simple feature comparison ,  it’s about finding a solution that aligns with a strategic vision for talent development and employee experience. Modern enterprises are turning onboarding into a competitive advantage. They recognize that in a tight labor market, providing a world-class onboarding experience can boost engagement, shorten time-to-productivity, and significantly improve retention. In fact, employees who have a positive onboarding experience are nearly 70% more likely to stay with a company for three years or more, and are substantially more committed and engaged from the outset. By 2026, onboarding software needs to enable this level of impact ,  by connecting systems, people, and insights in one cohesive platform.
The Strategic ROI of Onboarding
Transforming employee experience into long-term business value

The 2026 Shift: Moving from a "One-Time Event" ➡️ "Ongoing Strategic Journey"
For decision-makers like CHROs and L&D Directors, a value-first, ecosystem-oriented approach is key. The ideal solution will plug into your digital HR environment, automate the mundane, and surface the human touches that truly matter. It will provide the data to prove ROI (in retention saved, performance gained) and the flexibility to adapt as your organization evolves. Most importantly, it will help transform onboarding from a one-time orientation event into an ongoing journey of learning and integration. Enterprises that get this right will not only save costs and reduce turnover ,  they will cultivate a workforce that is confident, connected, and ready to drive the business forward. In the dynamic world of 2026, that is the ultimate return on investing in best-in-class onboarding software.
Elevating Your Onboarding Strategy with TechClass
Meeting the diverse requirements of a modern onboarding program, from AI-driven personalization to seamless compliance, requires more than just a digital checklist. It demands a robust infrastructure that can automate administrative workflows while delivering an engaging, human-centric experience. Without the right technology, balancing these strategic needs against the daily volume of new hires can quickly become overwhelming for HR teams.

FAQ
Why is onboarding considered a strategic investment in 2026?
Onboarding is a strategic investment because losing new hires is costly; replacing an employee can cost three to four times their salary. Strong processes improve new-hire retention by 82% and productivity by over 70%. In an era of talent shortages and hybrid work, effective onboarding is a must-have business strategy to secure talent and accelerate time-to-competency, impacting long-term business outcomes.
What are the benefits of seamless integration with HR and IT systems for onboarding software?
Seamless integration with HRIS, payroll, and IT systems eliminates redundant data entry and reduces errors. It ensures smooth data flow and automates cross-functional processes like IT provisioning, preventing productivity lags. This prepares everything behind the scenes, allowing new employees to focus on learning and people, leading to greater efficiency and consistency for the organization and a unified, professional welcome.
How does modern onboarding software personalize the new hire experience?
Modern onboarding software delivers personalized, role-based journeys tailored to each role, department, and individual background. It curates relevant training modules and milestones, driving faster time-to-productivity. AI-powered platforms dynamically adjust content based on experience or learning pace, ensuring the "just right" amount of information. This fosters engagement and demonstrates the company's investment in the new hire's success.
Why are pre-boarding engagement tools essential for new hires?
Pre-boarding engagement tools are essential because onboarding now begins before the first day. They keep future hires warm and excited by sending welcome portals or early checklists, maintaining candidate commitment, and drastically reducing "no-shows" (up to 50% of candidates may renege). These early touchpoints help new employees feel valued and prepared, lowering anxiety and improving retention for the enterprise.
How can AI-powered features enhance the onboarding process?
AI-powered features enhance onboarding through chatbots and virtual assistants that answer common new-hire questions 24/7, reducing HR load. AI can also personalize content delivery by recommending resources or adjusting pacing based on individual progress. Furthermore, predictive analytics can flag potential performance or retention risks early, allowing for timely intervention. This makes high-touch onboarding scalable and cost-effective.
What is the importance of inclusive and accessible design in onboarding software?
Inclusive and accessible design ensures the onboarding platform accommodates different languages, cultures, and abilities. Multilingual support and accessibility features like WCAG compliance, screen reader compatibility, and closed captioning are crucial. This makes every single new hire feel welcome and supported, leveraging contributions from a diverse workforce and preventing disengagement. It's a must-have for enterprises striving to be employers of choice.

How to onboard (great) product managers (so they can be awesome)
How to onboard (great) product managers (so they can be awesome)
Bilal Aslam
Bilal Aslam
A friend was recently complaining about his product management team not scaling - he was stepping into too many fires, and felt that decisions were churning. How could the product team have doubled in size, he asked, yet its effectiveness had seemingly declined? The answer, as I told him, may lie in how you onboard new product managers.
In this blog post, I will share a very specific approach to onboarding PMs that I learned at Databricks. Since this approach is shared by our engineering team, you should be able to apply it across all of R&D. I hope it helps you along your own journey and sheds some insights into this truly singular company's culture. I did not come up with this approach, I just have the privilege of sharing it with you.
"Into the deep end" doesn't really work
At Microsoft, where I was an intern in 2004, the philosophy was to throw hapless interns "into the deep end". Maybe it didn't help that we were housed in what was affectionately known as the "fish bowl" - a small, circular room stacked with PCs kept uncomfortably hot thanks to dodgy air conditioning. My first "spec" (PRD) review was an unmitigated disaster - I had no idea what I was doing, and the engineering team was kind enough to let me know just as much.
I eventually learned the ropes but the reality is that I stumbled, bumped and bounced my way through product management until I learned how to do the basics. "You're just drinking from the firehose!", I was told by mentors. There is a some truth to this statement - it is important to make (reversible) mistakes early in your career and learn from them, so you don't make them later on when the stakes are much higher.
Yet, I could never shake the feeling that my progression as a product manager felt like as much chance as design. Is there a better way to onboard product managers so they are more likely to succeed?
An onboarding process maximizes chances of success
At Databricks, I learned a specific approach to onboarding new product managers. I believe this approach is objectively superior and leads to measurably better outcomes compared to the alternatives. Why do we do things this way?
We assume that we are hiring incredibly smart, motivated people who want to do the right thing. However, even if they are domain experts, they know very little about our customers or product. They have certainly not sat in on sales calls or helped a customer debug a real problem! Is it fair to ask them to have the impact they desire without knowing any of this? How likely is their first PRD to be customer-obsessed, crisp and relevant if you have never shown them how to write a small PRD really well first?
The deliberate onboarding process I am going to describe below solves this exact problem.
Write an onboarding document
A well-designed and intentional onboarding process starts with an onboarding document for every new new hire. This document contains just enough context to get the employee up and running, and orients them in their team. I include the following sections:
 Your job: what is the actual job? Be specific, and limit it to three or four bullet points e.g. "The most important part of your job is to understand customer requirements". What are the outcomes I am looking for? Similarly, be specific e.g. "Raise NPS for this product by X points". Now your new hire knows what to do and how they will be measured.
 Quick start: a checklist of immediate team members to meet and reference docs to read. This helps them get up and running on Day 1.
 Communication channels: email lists and Slack channels to join and meetings to attend. Be stingy in adding new hires to meetings.
 PM reading: we have a library of articles about what it means to be a PM at Databricks. This takes a few hours to read. I read these articles out loud with new hires, and I also encourage them to read these articles offline.
 Week 1, 2, 3 breakdown: Give specific and fun activities to the new hire every week. The emphasis is on learning and fun. I ask new hires to focus on just one thing in Week 1: set up your accounts and laptop and meet people. Week 2 is about using the product and documenting their experience in a document that will be shared broadly. Week 3 is about getting on as many customer calls as possible. At this point, most new PMs will just tag along on existing calls, but the goal is to get them used to talking to customers.
 Side quests: This is a fun one - I like to give each new hire a purely optional side quest e.g. find a bug in documentation and fix it, or build a real-time data pipeline.
Importantly, none of the activities in the first few weeks have immediate business impact. They're purely focused on learning. Ask the new hire to keep an onboarding log so they can make the process easier for the next employee.
Do small very well things during onboarding
"Do small things very well" is a mantra I repeat many times to new PMs. This is the next phase of onboarding. An example of a "small thing done very well" could be writing a tiny one-page PRD about a UX nit, or about writing down and sharing great notes from a customer meeting.
This can feel counterintuitive for highly-motivated, self-driven personalities. They want to jump right in and solve hard problems, dammit! Setting this expectation is the kindest thing you can do to them. It creates space for them to learn the company's culture and way of doing business without worrying about immediate impact.
This approach has two benefits. First and most importantly, it teaches new PMs how to create excellent work product so their larger, more ambitious work will also be excellent. Second, their peers in engineering, design etc. gain faith in them and learn how to work with them. You have now set them up for success.
Onboarding requires immense amounts of work from hiring managers
At Databricks, hiring does not stop when a candidate signs. It stops when the new hire is onboarded successfully. Practically, this approach aligns incentives between the the new hire, their manager and the company. Depending on the role and experience, we can expect new hires to be "onboarding" for 6 to 9 months.
Managers have to commit to spending an inordinate amount of time with new hires to onboard them successfully. For example, I will typically spend an hour a day with a new hire for the first 3 weeks. No question is off limits. I've done everything from whiteboarding the evolution of data platforms to taking new hires on sales calls and explaining the informal org chart.
The message to hiring managers is that other than hiring great people, onboarding them is the highest leverage activity they can do. Standing meetings will still be there a month from now. However, if they manage to onboard successfully, their workload will actually decrease and their team will be much more effective.
Onboarding is the key to scaling product teams
This is the final insight I have from the onboarding process at Databricks. Other then hiring great PMs, onboarding them successfully is really the key to scaling your product team. If, as a product leader, you find yourself firefighting and unhappy about the effectiveness of your team, ask yourself if you and your managers are running a great onboarding process. The problem may very well lie there.
Databricks is hiring!
Speaking of hiring - Databricks is hiring product managers (and engineers!) in San Francisco, Seattle, Amsterdam and Berlin. If you are interested, drop me a note. I'd love to hear from you.

MentorCruise
Build an Onboarding Program That Gets New Hires Productive Fast
I have seen this gap firsthand through over 12,000 mentorships facilitated on MentorCruise - the people who struggle most in new roles are not lacking talent. They are lacking guidance during the most vulnerable phase of a job transition.
In this guide, I will walk you through the stages, frameworks, and strategies behind onboarding programs that actually work. More importantly, I will show you how mentorship accelerates every phase of onboarding, and what you can do to take ownership of your own experience - whether your company has a formal program or not.
Why Work With an Onboarding Program
An onboarding program exists because starting a new job is one of the hardest professional transitions you will face, and doing it without structure sets you up for failure. The difference between an employee who hits their stride in 30 days versus one still floundering at 90 days almost always comes down to the quality of their onboarding experience.
What Is an Onboarding Program, Really?
An onboarding program is the structured process of integrating a new employee into an organization - covering everything from paperwork and orientation to role training, cultural integration, and ongoing development. It is not the same as orientation, which is typically a one-day event focused on logistics like signing documents, getting your laptop, and learning where the coffee machine is. Onboarding is the longer arc. It can span weeks, months, or even a full year.
And to answer a question I see constantly: does onboarding mean you got hired? Yes. If a company has started your onboarding, you have the job. Onboarding is what happens after the offer letter is signed. Some companies blur the line by sending pre-boarding materials before your start date, but that is still part of the onboarding process - not a sign that your offer is conditional.
How Mentorship Drives Onboarding Success
A mentor accelerates onboarding because they give you something no training manual or orientation session can - context. They help you understand the unwritten rules, the political dynamics, the shortcuts that only come from experience. Research published in the Academy of Management Journal consistently shows that employees with mentors during onboarding report higher job satisfaction, faster skill acquisition, and stronger organizational commitment.
Why do onboarding programs fail without mentorship? Because they rely on information transfer alone. You get the policies, the org chart, the product documentation. What you do not get is someone who can translate all of that into practical guidance for your specific role. A mentor fills that gap. They have already managed the same environment and can tell you what actually matters versus what is noise.
The difference between mentor-led onboarding and self-directed onboarding is significant. Self-directed onboarding puts the full burden on you to figure things out, ask the right questions, and build relationships from scratch. Mentor-led onboarding gives you a guide. Someone who checks in, anticipates your blind spots, and helps you avoid the mistakes that typically cost new hires months of momentum.
The Pain Points That Make Onboarding So Hard
New job overwhelm is real, and it hits hardest in the first two weeks. You are learning new systems, new people, new expectations, and new norms simultaneously. The cognitive load is enormous. If your onboarding program lacks guidance and no mentor is assigned, you are essentially managing a new country without a map.
Here are the most common onboarding pain points I hear about from people who eventually find their way to MentorCruise:
A structured onboarding program with a dedicated mentor or buddy addresses every single one of these. The mentor becomes your feedback loop, your expectations translator, and your social connector all at once.
TL;DR - An onboarding program is the structured process of integrating new hires - it goes far beyond day-one orientation - The 5 stages cover pre-boarding through ongoing development, and the 5 C's (Compliance, Clarification, Culture, Connection, Check-back) give you a framework to evaluate any program - Mentorship is the single biggest accelerator of onboarding success - employees with mentors ramp up faster and stay longer - If your company's onboarding falls short, you can build your own plan by finding an external mentor, starting at $120/month on MentorCruise - Every MentorCruise mentorship includes a free trial, async messaging, and the ability to cancel anytime
What to Expect From Onboarding Program Sessions
An effective onboarding program follows a predictable arc - from pre-boarding logistics through deep integration and ongoing development - and knowing the stages helps you gauge whether your company's program is actually doing its job.
The 5 Stages of the Onboarding Process
The five stages of onboarding give you a roadmap for what should happen and when:
The 4 Steps of Onboarding
If five stages feel granular, here is the simpler four-step framework that many organizations use: orientation, role training, integration, and follow-up. The key difference is that this model collapses pre-boarding into orientation and emphasizes follow-up as a distinct step - checking back with new hires at 30, 60, and 90 days to make sure nothing has fallen through the cracks.
The 5 C's of Onboarding
The 5 C's give you a lens for evaluating the quality of any onboarding program:
If your company's onboarding program hits all five C's, you are in good shape. If it only covers compliance and some clarification - which is unfortunately common - you have a gap that mentorship can fill.
What an Effective Onboarding Program Should Include
An effective onboarding program for new employees includes clear milestones, a dedicated point of contact (ideally a mentor or buddy), regular check-ins, and opportunities to give feedback. It should be structured enough to provide direction but flexible enough to adapt to individual needs.
Here is the onboarding program example I recommend to anyone joining a new company: map your first 90 days into three phases. Days 1-30 are for learning - absorb everything, ask every question, and take notes obsessively. Days 31-60 are for contributing - start taking ownership of small projects and build on the relationships you have formed. Days 61-90 are for leading - propose improvements, take on bigger responsibilities, and establish yourself as someone who adds value.
That template works whether your company has a formal onboarding program or not. And if they do not? That is where finding your own mentor becomes non-negotiable.
How to Get the Most Out of Your Onboarding
You can accelerate your onboarding by being proactive rather than passive. Here are the strategies I have seen work best across thousands of mentorships:
Ask for a mentor or buddy. If your company does not assign one, request one. Frame it as wanting to understand the team culture and learn from someone who has been there. Most managers appreciate the initiative.
Create your own onboarding plan when the company lacks one. Write down your questions, identify the people who can answer them, and set your own 30-60-90 day goals. Share this plan with your manager. You will immediately stand out as organized and self-directed.
Find an external mentor during onboarding. If internal options are limited, a mentor outside your company offers a different kind of value - objective perspective without organizational politics. On MentorCruise, many of our mentors specialize in leadership coaching sessions and management coaching sessions that directly apply to managing a new role.
Document everything. Keep a running document of what you are learning, who you are meeting, and what questions come up. This becomes your personal onboarding reference and shows your manager that you are engaged.
How to Choose the Right Onboarding Program
Start by evaluating whether the program matches the complexity of the role and the needs of the new hire - a one-size-fits-all approach is one of the most common reasons onboarding fails.
Structured vs. Unstructured Onboarding
A structured onboarding program follows a defined timeline with clear milestones, assigned mentors or buddies, regular check-ins, and documented expectations. An unstructured approach is more of a "figure it out" model - you get a desk, a login, and maybe a lunch with your new team.
The data overwhelmingly favors structured onboarding. Employees who go through structured programs are 58% more likely to still be with the organization after three years, according to SHRM research. Unstructured onboarding often leads to the pain points I described earlier - confusion, isolation, and slow ramp-up times.
But here is the nuance: structured does not mean rigid. The best onboarding programs build in flexibility for individual differences. Some people learn best by doing. Others need to observe first. A good program accommodates both.
How Long Should an Onboarding Program Last?
An effective onboarding program should last at least 90 days, though the best ones extend to six months or a full year. The common mistake is treating onboarding as a one-week event. Research consistently shows that the "onboarding cliff" - where support drops off but competency gaps remain - happens around week three or four. Programs that end at orientation miss the entire integration and development phases where real productivity gains happen.
At MentorCruise, we designed our platform around long-term mentorship relationships, not one-off calls, because we saw this pattern play out constantly. People do not need a single advice session during their first week. They need an ongoing relationship with someone who can help them manage the challenges that emerge over months.
Red Flags in an Onboarding Program
Watch for these warning signs:
Questions to Ask Before Starting
If you are evaluating a new role or have already accepted an offer, ask these questions about the onboarding program:
These questions signal that you are serious about ramping up quickly and help you identify gaps you may need to fill on your own.
Onboarding Program Costs and Investment
The cost of a bad onboarding program is far higher than the cost of a good one - replacing an employee who leaves within the first year can cost 50-200% of their annual salary.
What Companies Spend on Onboarding
Organizations typically invest $1,000 to $5,000 per new hire on onboarding, depending on the role's complexity and the program's depth. That number includes training materials, mentor time, software licenses, and administrative overhead. Companies that invest more in onboarding see faster time-to-productivity and lower turnover - it is one of the clearest ROI calculations in human resources.
What You Should Invest in Your Own Onboarding
If your company's onboarding falls short, investing in your own support system is one of the smartest career moves you can make. Professional mentorship through MentorCruise starts at $120 per month - which is 70% cheaper than comparable coaching rates that often run $300-500 per hour. That monthly subscription gives you regular sessions, async messaging between sessions, and ongoing access to your mentor's expertise.
We accept fewer than 5% of mentor applicants because quality matters more than quantity. Every mentor on the platform has been vetted for professional experience, communication skills, and mentoring track record. Our mentors maintain a 97% satisfaction rate with a 4.9 out of 5 average rating across thousands of reviews.
And there is no risk in trying. Every mentor on MentorCruise offers a free trial session so you can test the fit before committing any money. If it is not the right match, you can cancel anytime - no long-term contracts, no cancellation fees.
Evaluating the Return
The return on mentorship during onboarding shows up in three ways: faster ramp-up time (getting productive weeks or months sooner), higher job satisfaction (reducing the risk of early turnover), and stronger professional relationships (building a network from day one instead of month six).
I built MentorCruise after watching my peers spend $20,000 on bootcamps without landing jobs. The courses ended, the mentors disappeared, and people were left alone right when they needed guidance most. The same pattern plays out in corporate onboarding. The formal program ends, and suddenly you are expected to be fully productive with zero ongoing support. A mentor changes that equation entirely.
Frequently Asked Questions
How much does an onboarding program cost?
Company-run onboarding programs typically cost $1,000-5,000 per new hire, though you do not pay for those directly - your employer does. If you are looking to supplement your onboarding with external mentorship, MentorCruise starts at $120 per month, which includes regular sessions and async messaging. Traditional coaching rates range from $300-500 per hour, making subscription mentorship significantly more accessible for new hires investing in their own ramp-up.
How do I know if I need an onboarding program?
You likely need more onboarding support if you feel lost or overwhelmed in your first few weeks, your company did not assign a mentor or buddy, you are unclear on what success looks like in your role, or you are struggling to build relationships with your new team. If you are setting goals and not hitting them, or if you keep running into the same roadblocks without knowing who to ask, those are strong signals that mentorship would help.
What should I look for when choosing an onboarding program?
Look for clear structure with defined milestones at 30, 60, and 90 days. Check whether the program includes a mentor or buddy assignment - this single element predicts success better than almost any other factor. Evaluate whether the program covers all 5 C's (Compliance, Clarification, Culture, Connection, Check-back) rather than just paperwork and policies. Red flags include no assigned point of contact, no follow-up after the first week, and a one-size-fits-all approach that ignores role-specific needs.
How long until I see results from an onboarding program?
Most people notice improved confidence and clarity within the first two weeks of a structured onboarding program or mentorship relationship. Measurable outcomes - like hitting performance targets, earning positive feedback, or completing projects independently - typically emerge by month two or three. The full impact of a good onboarding program shows up in retention and promotion data at the six-month to one-year mark. With a dedicated mentor, the timeline compresses because you skip the trial-and-error phase that slows most new hires down.

Employee Onboarding Guide
Onboarding Process
Last Updated: May 15th, 2024
While there are many ways to design an onboarding program, some components are integral to the process. By incorporating steps like preboarding, orientation, foundation-building, and mentoring/buddy systems, you can help ensure employees are set up for success in their new roles.
Onboarding Guide Navigation
> Onboarding Process
Preboarding
Some organizations want to begin the onboarding process after the offer is accepted but before the actual start date. In these situations, employers may want to develop strategies to link new employees to the organization. Examples include the following:
Orientation
New-hire orientation is a formal event to introduce the new employee to the organization's structure, vision, mission and values; review the employee handbook and highlight major policies; complete required employee paperwork; review pertinent administrative procedures; and provide mandatory training. This process can overload a new employee with information and is therefore best done over a few days or a week, if possible.
More Orientation Resources:
Foundation Building
Throughout the onboarding process, an organization's unique pillars of culture, mission, employee value proposition, brand and other relevant foundations must be lived and conveyed consistently. New hires will not absorb this in the first week or first month; onboarding takes many months to learn and apply. Identifying the enduring values and aspirational goals unique to the organization will help guide the development of an onboarding program.
Gallup's Creating an Exceptional Onboarding Journey for Your New Employees suggests onboarding should take up the better part of a year. Gallup has discovered that there are five main questions asked during that time that, when addressed, lead to an exceptional onboarding program that sets up employees for success.
The explanations and examples in Gallup's study provide detailed guidance on how an organization might provide answers to these pivotal questions that will support your onboarding foundations.
More Foundation-Building Resources:
Mentoring and Buddy Systems
Many organizations offer a formal or informal mentoring or buddy system to support the new employee during the onboarding period. Mentors and buddies may be volunteers or selected by the department manager or HR professional. In some companies, recent hires are assigned to be buddies, as they have firsthand knowledge of what has helped them most.
Generally, the role of the mentor or buddy is to offer the new employee a connection to someone who can guide them but is not in a position of direct authority or acting in an official capacity. The mentor or buddy may be responsible for such mundane tasks as giving directions to the restrooms or cafeteria or instructions on parking guidelines, or he or she may be involved in helping the employee understand the nuances of working in the organization (e.g., the hot buttons for those in executive leadership or getting projects approved).
Mentors and buddies may be teamed with a new employee for a day, week, month or even a year, depending on the length of the formal onboarding program and the personal relationship that develops, especially in more-informal arrangements. Learn more about the advantages of instituting a buddy system at your organization.
Continue Learning About Onboarding

How to Plan for a New Hire: Timelines, Stakeholders, and Onboarding
How to Plan for a New Hire: Timelines, Stakeholders, and Onboarding
Circle
Circle
12K followers
12K followers
In today's competitive STEM industries, bringing a new hire on board is more than just filling a vacancy—it's about seamlessly integrating a valuable team member into your organisation. At CSR Group, we understand the complexities of this process and are here to guide you through planning for your new hire effectively.
The Timeline: A Strategic Approach
4-6 Weeks Before Start Date:
 Finalise the job description and responsibilities
 Identify key stakeholders in the onboarding process
 Begin preparing necessary equipment and access credentials
2-3 Weeks Before:
 Send a welcome package, including first-day instructions and company information
 Set up the new hire's workstation and necessary accounts
 Brief team members on the new hire's role and start date
1 Week Before:
 Confirm start date and first-day agenda with the new hire
 Finalise the onboarding schedule for the first week
 Assign a mentor or buddy to support the new hire
First Day:
 Warmly welcome the new hire and provide a tour of the facilities
 Complete necessary paperwork and provide security access
 Introduce the new hire to their team and key colleagues
First Week:
 Conduct orientation sessions on company culture, policies, and procedures
 Begin role-specific training
 Schedule check-ins with the manager and HR representative
First Month:
 Continue role-specific training and integration into team projects
 Conduct weekly check-ins to address any concerns or questions
 Begin setting performance expectations and goals
Key Stakeholders in the Onboarding Process
1. HR Department: Coordinates the overall onboarding process, handles paperwork, and ensures compliance with company policies.
2. Direct Manager: Provides role-specific guidance, sets performance expectations, and integrates the new hire into the team.
3. IT Department: Sets up necessary technology and access rights.
4. Mentor/Buddy: Offers day-to-day support and helps the new hire navigate company culture.
5. Team Members: Welcome the new hire and assist in their integration into team dynamics.
6. Senior Leadership: May participate in welcoming activities to demonstrate company values and vision.
Effective Onboarding Strategies
1. Pre-boarding: Engage your new hire before their first day. Use CSR Group's Virtual Bench Plus to provide access to company information and initial training materials.
2. Personalisation: Tailor the onboarding experience to the new hire's role and background. Our TAAS (Talent as a Service) solution can help create customised onboarding plans.
3. Clear Communication: Provide a clear onboarding schedule and set of expectations. Regularly check in with the new hire to address any concerns.
4. Gradual Integration: Avoid information overload by spacing out training and introductions over the first few weeks.
5. Cultural Immersion: Help the new hire understand and integrate into your company culture through team-building activities and mentor support.
6. Feedback Loop: Regularly solicit feedback from the new hire about their onboarding experience to continuously improve your process.
The Long-term View
Remember, onboarding doesn't end after the first week or even the first month. It's an ongoing process that can last up to a year as the new hire fully integrates into their role and the company culture.
By investing time and resources into a comprehensive onboarding plan, you're setting the stage for higher employee engagement, improved retention rates, and ultimately, a more productive and satisfied workforce.
At CSR Group, we're committed to helping you navigate every stage of the hiring process, from initial talent pooling through to long-term employee development. Our innovative solutions like Virtual Bench Plus and TAAS are designed to streamline your hiring and onboarding processes, ensuring you can attract and retain the best STEM talent in an increasingly competitive market.
Ready to revolutionise your hiring and onboarding strategy? Get in touch with our expert team today to discover how we can support your organisation's growth and success.

Smiling woman using a mobile phone with a bar chart icon, representing employee onboarding platform features and analytics for tracking new hire progress.
Guide page discussing onboarding challenges from HR and new hires' perspectives.

Who are the most important stakeholders in the onboarding process?

.jpg)
Copied!
A strong start is everything. New hires make up their minds fast. Research shows 70% decide if their job is the right fit within the first month, and 29% know within just one week! If the role doesn’t meet expectations or they don’t click with their team, they’re far more likely to leave early or never fully settle in.
Effective onboarding requires more than just HR's involvement. When managers, buddies, and other key players get involved, new hires get a better sense of company culture and build real relationships with their team.
So, what does a great onboarding experience actually look like? At its core, it's about helping new hires build authentic connections with their team from the start. And the best way to do that? Get multiple stakeholders intentionally involved from day one.
‍
Why should you include different stakeholders during onboarding?
We’ve already talked about why onboarding shouldn’t be HR’s job alone. Now let’s dig a little deeper.
When HR has to do everything - from paperwork to office tours - things inevitably fall through the cracks. And if the rest of the team isn’t on the same page, onboarding falls flat. No surprise, then, that 65% of new hires don’t know who to ask questions to, 62% feel lost about company products, and 51% struggle with tech issues (BambooHR).
Instead, it’s best to divide and conquer: help new hires settle in by connecting them with the right people across the company. When managers actively participate, employees are 3.4x more likely to say their onboarding was succesful (Gallup). And 56% of new hires credit a buddy, even just one meeting, for helping them ramp up faster (Microsoft)
HR still plays a key role in handling the basics. But when managers explain job expectations, buddies answer daily questions, and colleagues share insider tips, we create an onboarding experience that actually works for new hires.
So, who exactly should pitch in and how? Let’s break it down.
Infographic titled 'Roles within the onboarding process' with illustrations of six key roles: HR, Manager, Buddy, IT Department, The Team, and The New Hire.
Who are the (key) stakeholders?
Different departments and people should be involved in the onboarding process since they each have their own added value. Let's break down the key players who can make onboarding truly effective, along with practical ways to set them up for success:
1. Manager
[.callout-small] Team managers provide role-specific guidance, set clear expectations, and help new hires integrate into the team’s daily work and culture. [.callout-small]
‍
The team manager plays the most critical role in a new hire's success. After all, they know the role best and will work closely with the employee daily. While HR oversees the overall process, managers provide the day-to-day guidance that really helps new hires get up to speed.
Managers help new employees understand their specific responsibilities and how their role fits within the team. They set clear expectations and performance goals, offering regular feedback and support during those crucial first months. Working together with HR, they can help develop the new hire's 30-60-90 day plan to create a structured roadmap for success.
Just as importantly, managers help new hires feel at home in their team. By organizing introductions and facilitating early collaboration, they create opportunities for relationships to form naturally. This support helps new employees feel connected and confident from the start of their employee journey.
Read more about the importance of involving managers in the onboarding process
‍
How to support managers during onboarding
Make it easy for them to succeed by:
Most managers want to onboard well but lack time. Automating reminders and providing templates reduces their mental load while ensuring consistency.
Remember: while HR provides the framework, it's the manager who brings onboarding to life in ways that matter most to the new hire's daily experience.
2. HR
[.callout-small] HR oversees the onboarding process, handles the paperwork, and ensures consistency, so every new hire can hit the ground running. [.callout-small]
‍
In most organizations, HR (or an onboarding specialist) leads the entire onboarding process. When digital tools are involved, they’re the ones setting up the system, assigning tasks, and keeping things on track.
They also take care of the key administrative tasks so that everything runs smoothly for the new hires. This includes paperwork, introducing company policies and culture, and more. Many of these administrative tasks are taken care of before the new hire’s first day to prepare for a smooth experience.
Often, HR is also the go-to contact person for any questions or concerns new employees may have. Their focus? Crafting an experience that makes people feel welcomed, not overwhelmed, because great onboarding is the simplest way to stop early turnover.
While other stakeholders contribute their unique perspectives, HR provides the essential framework that ensures consistent, organization-wide onboarding. They coordinate managers, buddies, and peers while maintaining oversight of the entire process.
Some tools to help you with success:
Accelerate new hire performance with a structured plan that starts before day one and delivers real results.
3. Buddy
[.callout-small] Buddies offer informal support, answer day-to-day questions and make new hires feel socially comfortable and connected in their new work environment [.callout-small]
‍
An onboarding buddy is a colleague who provides day-to-day support, complementing the manager's role-specific training and HR's administrative functions. They focus on the human side of onboarding - explaining unwritten rules, sharing where to find the best lunch, and answering all those small but important questions new hires might feel awkward asking their manager.
The numbers show just how valuable this is: 56% of new hires report becoming productive faster after just one buddy meeting. Meeting 2-3 times boosts that to 73%. For those connecting 4-8 times, 97% experience faster productivity (Microsoft).
Having a friendly colleague to turn to makes all the difference in those critical first weeks. They help new employees feel connected in what can otherwise be a lonely transition period.
How to support your buddies?
Your onboarding buddies need support, too. Here's how to equip them:
1. Build a structured buddy program  
‍  
Create clear guidelines with roles and expectations using our Onboarding Buddy Toolbox.
2. Train buddies on their role
‍Share our article How to Be a Great Onboarding Buddy, which includes:
3. Provide practical tools
Find more tips in the blog: how to implement a buddy program.
4. IT Department
[.callout-small] IT ensures that new hires have access to necessary tools, systems, and tech-support from day one [.callout-small]  
‍
Nothing sabotages a new hire's first day like missing equipment or login issues. Yet this happens surprisingly often, causing significant frustrations for new employees. In fact, research shows these rank among new hires' biggest pain points (BambooHR):
This is why IT plays such a critical role in successful onboarding. When involved early, they ensure all devices are properly set up, system access is ready on day one, and security protocols are explained. Just as importantly, they become the dedicated support channel for resolving any technical challenges that emerge during the transition.
Help them be prepared by offering checklists and assigning tasks with clear deadlines. Most importantly, make sure managers proactively communicate exactly what system accesses and tools the new hire will need.
Because when IT, HR, and managers coordinate effectively, what's traditionally been a major frustration point becomes one less thing for new hires to worry about.
5. The team
[.callout-small]The team brings onboarding to life through daily interactions that help new employees integrate [.callout-small]  
‍
While buddies provide one-on-one support, it's the entire team that truly makes a new hire feel at home. Colleagues create the lived experience of your company culture through everyday interactions - something no handbook can capture.
The numbers prove how much these connections matter: 87% of new hires hope to make friends at work and 93% want to shadow colleagues (BambooHR). Perhaps most telling? Nearly 40% of employees say their coworkers are the top reason they love their company (Virgin Pulse).
These human connections happen when teams:
Highlight to your organization the importance of giving genuine attention to new colleagues. And create free space in the calendars of both new hires and their teammates for coffee moments.
6. The new hire
[.callout-small] Onboarding isn't a one-way street. A new hire's curiosity and proactive approach are just as crucial as having a well-prepared team for successful integration [.callout-small]
‍
And of course, there is the new hire themself! For a successful onboarding experience, new hires must play an active role in onboarding. Alongside the helpful guidance they receive, it’s also important for them to take initiative to learn more about the organization.
They can do this by engaging with onboarding materials, asking questions, building relationships with colleagues, and learning about the company culture and their role.
How do you best support new employees?‍
While onboarding is all about helping new colleagues, the key is not to do everything for them. Instead, encourage them to take initiative themselves.
Encourage new colleagues to proactively schedule coffee chats with teammates. This helps them expand their network while gaining insights into team dynamics.
Involve them in creating their 30-60-90 day plan too. What do they want to learn? What are their personal goals? This gives them ownership of their onboarding journey.
Don't forget to regularly ask for their feedback - what's working well and what's missing? As they reflect on their progress, you'll gain valuable insights to continuously improve the process."
Here you will find tips on how to get feedback from your new hires.
‍
How can other roles support the onboarding process?
CEO
[.callout-small] The CEO brings the vision to life – not just by communicating it, but by leading by example. They help new hires feel welcomed and connected to the organization’s purpose [.callout-small]
‍
The CEO plays a unique role in onboarding by bringing the company’s vision to life. By personally sharing the organization's purpose, values, and strategic goals, they help new hires understand how their daily work contributes to something bigger.
More than just explaining what we do, the CEO answers why it matters. This connection transforms onboarding from learning tasks to understanding impact.
How can CEOs make an impact:
A personal touch from leadership reinforces the company culture and shows that every employee matters.
Marketing & Communications
[.callout-small] Marketing & Communications turns onboarding into an enjoyable, engaging first experience that motivates new hires to look forward to what's ahead and make information stick [.callout-small]  
‍
Onboarding shouldn't just be effective - it should be enjoyable too. Especially since much of it happens during the new hire's personal time, even before their first workday. This is where Marketing & Communications comes in. They transform dull information into an engaging first impression by:
Their mission? To ensure new employees begin their journey with enthusiasm and excitement.
Recruitment/Talent Acquisition
[.callout-small] Recruiters form the bridge between hiring and onboarding, and make sure there’s a smooth transition by sharing candidate insights and setting the tone for a personalized welcome. [.callout-small]
Recruiters are the new hire’s first human connection to your company. They’ve built trust during the hiring process and understand what excites the candidate about the role. By involving TA early in onboarding, you:
Onboarding as a team
A strong onboarding process is a shared effort that involves multiple stakeholders, each playing a unique and valuable role. From HR and managers to buddies, IT, and even the CEO, every role helps new hires feel supported and connected. By working together, organizations can create a welcoming experience that sets employees up for long-term success.
